title.raw: 《大宛列傳》 Treatise on the Dayuan

sentences.zh: 大宛之跡，見自張騫。

translations.literal: The traces of Dayuan were first seen through Zhang Qian.
translations.idiomatic: What we know of Dayuan first came to light through Zhang Qian.

sentences.zh: 張騫，漢中人。

translations.literal: Zhang Qian was a man of Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was a native of Hanzhong.

sentences.zh: 建元中為郎。

translations.literal: During the Jianyuan era he served as a Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: During the Jianyuan era, he served as a Gentleman at court.

sentences.zh: 是時天子問匈奴降者，皆言匈奴破月氏王，以其頭為飲器，月氏遁逃而常怨仇匈奴，無與共擊之。

translations.literal: At that time the Son of Heaven questioned Xiongnu defectors, who all said the Xiongnu had defeated the Yuezhi king, made his skull into a drinking vessel, and the Yuezhi had fled but constantly harbored resentment against the Xiongnu, having no one to join them in attacking them.
translations.idiomatic: At that time, the Emperor questioned Xiongnu defectors, who all told the same story: the Xiongnu had defeated the king of the Yuezhi and fashioned his skull into a drinking vessel. The Yuezhi had fled, but they nursed an enduring hatred of the Xiongnu, though they had found no ally willing to join them in a strike against their enemy.

sentences.zh: 漢方欲事滅胡，聞此言，因欲通使。

translations.literal: Han was just then planning to destroy the Hu; hearing these words, they therefore wished to send an envoy to make contact.
translations.idiomatic: The Han were then seeking to destroy the Xiongnu. Upon hearing this, the court resolved to open diplomatic contact with the Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 道必更匈奴中，乃募能使者。

translations.literal: The road necessarily passed through Xiongnu territory, so they recruited someone able to serve as envoy.
translations.idiomatic: Since the route necessarily passed through Xiongnu territory, the court put out a call for a man capable of undertaking the mission.

sentences.zh: 騫以郎應募，使月氏，與堂邑氏（故）胡奴甘父俱出隴西。

translations.literal: Qian, as a Gentleman, answered the call, was sent to the Yuezhi, and together with Ganfu, a former Hu slave of the Tangyi clan, set out from Longxi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian, then serving as a Gentleman, volunteered for the mission. He was dispatched to the Yuezhi and set out from Longxi together with Ganfu, a former Xiongnu slave of the Tangyi clan.

sentences.zh: 經匈奴，匈奴得之，傳詣單于。

translations.literal: Passing through Xiongnu territory, the Xiongnu captured them and escorted them to the Chanyu.
translations.idiomatic: As they crossed Xiongnu territory, they were captured and brought before the Chanyu.

sentences.zh: 單于留之，曰：「月氏在吾北，漢何以得往使？

translations.literal: The Chanyu detained them and said: 'The Yuezhi are to my north. How can Han send envoys there?'
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu detained them, saying, "The Yuezhi lie to my north. By what right does Han send envoys through my domain?

sentences.zh: 吾欲使越，漢肯聽我乎？」

translations.literal: If I wished to send envoys to Yue, would Han permit me?
translations.idiomatic: If I wished to send envoys to Yue, would Han permit it?"

sentences.zh: 留騫十餘歲，與妻，有子，然騫持漢節不失。

translations.literal: He detained Qian for more than ten years, gave him a wife, and he had sons, yet Qian held the Han tally without losing it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was held captive for over ten years. He was given a wife and fathered children, yet through it all he kept hold of the Han envoy's tally and never relinquished it.

sentences.zh: 居匈奴中，益寬，騫因與其屬亡鄉月氏，西走數十日至大宛。

translations.literal: Living among the Xiongnu, as restrictions gradually loosened, Qian and his attendants escaped toward the Yuezhi, traveling west for tens of days until they reached Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: As his captivity among the Xiongnu grew more relaxed, Zhang Qian seized an opportunity to escape with his attendants toward the Yuezhi. They fled westward for some tens of days before reaching Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 大宛聞漢之饒財，欲通不得，見騫，喜，問曰：「若欲何之？」

translations.literal: Dayuan had heard of Han's abundant wealth and wished to make contact but could not; seeing Qian, they were pleased and asked: 'Where do you wish to go?'
translations.idiomatic: The people of Dayuan had long heard of the wealth of Han and wished to establish contact, though they had been unable to do so. When they saw Zhang Qian, they were delighted and asked, "Where are you headed?"

sentences.zh: 騫曰：「為漢使月氏，而為匈奴所閉道。

translations.literal: Qian said: 'I was sent by Han as envoy to the Yuezhi, but the Xiongnu blocked my way.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian replied, "I was sent by the Han court as an envoy to the Yuezhi, but the Xiongnu blocked my passage.

sentences.zh: 今亡，唯王使人導送我。

translations.literal: Now I have escaped; I only ask that the King send someone to guide and escort me.
translations.idiomatic: Now I have escaped. I ask only that Your Majesty send guides to escort me on my way.

sentences.zh: 誠得至，反漢，漢之賂遺王財物不可勝言。」

translations.literal: If I truly reach there and return to Han, the gifts and treasures Han will bestow upon the King will be beyond reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: If I succeed in reaching them and return to Han, the gifts and treasures the court will bestow upon Your Majesty will be beyond reckoning."

sentences.zh: 大宛以為然，遣騫，為發導繹，抵康居，康居傳致大月氏。

translations.literal: Dayuan considered this reasonable, dispatched Qian, and sent guides and interpreters who brought him to Kangju; Kangju in turn passed him on to the Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Dayuan agreed and sent Zhang Qian on his way with guides and interpreters, who brought him to Kangju. From there, Kangju passed him onward to the Great Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 大月氏王已為胡所殺，立其太子為王。

translations.literal: The Great Yuezhi king had already been killed by the Hu, and they had installed his crown prince as king.
translations.idiomatic: The king of the Great Yuezhi had already been killed by the Xiongnu, and the people had installed his crown prince as the new king.

sentences.zh: 既臣大夏而居，地肥饒，少寇，志安樂，又自以遠漢，殊無報胡之心。

translations.literal: Having already subjugated Daxia and settled there, the land was fertile and rich, with few raiders; their ambition was for ease and comfort, and considering themselves far from Han, they had absolutely no desire to take revenge on the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: Having already subjugated Bactria and settled in its territory, they found the land fertile and rich, with few threats from raiders. Content in their new prosperity and considering themselves far removed from Han, they had no desire whatsoever to seek vengeance against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 騫從月氏至大夏，竟不能得月氏要領。

translations.literal: Qian traveled from the Yuezhi to Daxia, but in the end could not obtain the Yuezhi's agreement to his proposal.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian traveled from the Yuezhi to Bactria, but in the end he could not win the Yuezhi over to his proposal.

sentences.zh: 留歲餘，還，并南山，欲從羌中歸，復為匈奴所得。

translations.literal: After staying over a year, he returned, traveling along the Southern Mountains, intending to go home through Qiang territory, but was again captured by the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: After remaining there for over a year, he set out for home, following the line of the Southern Mountains and intending to pass through Qiang territory. But once again the Xiongnu captured him.

sentences.zh: 留歲餘，單于死，左谷蠡王攻其太子自立，國內亂，騫與胡妻及堂邑父俱亡歸漢。

translations.literal: After being detained over a year, the Chanyu died; the Left Luli King attacked the crown prince and set himself up as ruler; the state fell into chaos, and Qian with his Hu wife and Tangyi's Ganfu together escaped and returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: He was held for over a year before the Chanyu died. The Left Luli King attacked the heir apparent and seized the throne for himself, plunging the realm into chaos. In the turmoil, Zhang Qian escaped with his Xiongnu wife and Ganfu and made his way back to Han.

sentences.zh: 漢拜騫為太中大夫，堂邑父為奉使君。

translations.literal: Han appointed Qian as Grand Palace Grandee and Tangyi's Ganfu as Lord Who Accompanied the Envoy.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court appointed Zhang Qian as Grand Palace Grandee, and conferred on Ganfu the title of Lord Who Accompanied the Envoy.

sentences.zh: 騫為人彊力，寬大信人，蠻夷愛之。

translations.literal: Qian as a person was strong and vigorous, broad-minded and trustworthy; the barbarians loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was a man of great physical strength, generous in spirit and true to his word. The peoples of the frontier held him in deep affection.

sentences.zh: 堂邑父故胡人，善射，窮急射禽獸給食。

translations.literal: Tangyi's Ganfu was originally a Hu person, skilled at archery; when in desperate straits he would shoot birds and beasts to provide food.
translations.idiomatic: Ganfu, originally a Xiongnu, was a skilled archer. Whenever they were in dire straits, he would hunt game to keep them fed.

sentences.zh: 初，騫行時百餘人，去十三歲，唯二人得還。

translations.literal: Originally, when Qian set out there were over a hundred men; after thirteen years away, only two returned.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Qian first set out, his party numbered over a hundred men. Thirteen years later, only two of them returned.

sentences.zh: 騫身所至者大宛、大月氏、大夏、康居，而傳聞其旁大國五六，具為天子言之。

translations.literal: The places Qian personally visited were Dayuan, the Great Yuezhi, Daxia, and Kangju; and he had heard reports of five or six great nations nearby, which he related in full to the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The lands Zhang Qian had visited in person were Dayuan, the Great Yuezhi, Bactria, and Kangju. He had also gathered reports of five or six other great states in the region, all of which he presented in full to the Emperor.

sentences.zh: 曰：

translations.literal: He said:
translations.idiomatic: He reported as follows:

sentences.zh: 大宛在匈奴西南，在漢正西，去漢可萬里。

translations.literal: Dayuan lies to the southwest of the Xiongnu, due west of Han, approximately ten thousand li from Han.
translations.idiomatic: Dayuan lies to the southwest of the Xiongnu and due west of Han, roughly ten thousand li distant.

sentences.zh: 其俗土著，耕田，田稻麥。

translations.literal: Their custom is to be settled; they farm the land, cultivating rice and wheat.
translations.idiomatic: The people are settled farmers who cultivate rice and wheat.

sentences.zh: 有蒲陶酒。

translations.literal: They have grape wine.
translations.idiomatic: They produce grape wine.

sentences.zh: 多善馬，馬汗血，其先天馬子也。

translations.literal: They have many fine horses; the horses sweat blood, and their ancestors were the offspring of heavenly horses.
translations.idiomatic: They possess many fine horses whose coats sweat blood, said to be descended from the heavenly horses.

sentences.zh: 有城郭屋室。

translations.literal: They have walled cities and houses.
translations.idiomatic: They have walled cities and proper dwellings.

sentences.zh: 其屬邑大小七十餘城，眾可數十萬。

translations.literal: Their subordinate towns and cities, large and small, number over seventy, with a population of several hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Their territory encompasses over seventy towns and cities of various sizes, with a total population of several hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 其兵弓矛騎射。

translations.literal: Their weapons are bows, spears, and mounted archery.
translations.idiomatic: Their soldiers fight with bows, spears, and mounted archery.

sentences.zh: 其北則康居，西則大月氏，西南則大夏，東北則烏孫，東則扜穼、于窴。

translations.literal: To their north lies Kangju, to the west the Great Yuezhi, to the southwest Daxia, to the northeast the Wusun, and to the east Yumi and Khotan.
translations.idiomatic: To the north lies Kangju; to the west, the Great Yuezhi; to the southwest, Bactria; to the northeast, the Wusun; and to the east, Yumi and Khotan.

sentences.zh: 于窴之西，則水皆西流，注西海；

translations.literal: West of Khotan, all waters flow westward and empty into the Western Sea;
translations.idiomatic: West of Khotan, all the rivers flow westward into the Western Sea.

sentences.zh: 其東水東流，注鹽澤。

translations.literal: To its east, the waters flow eastward and empty into the Salt Marsh.
translations.idiomatic: To the east of Khotan, the rivers flow eastward into the Salt Marsh.

sentences.zh: 鹽澤潛行地下，其南則河源出焉。

translations.literal: The Salt Marsh flows underground; to its south, the source of the Yellow River emerges.
translations.idiomatic: The waters of the Salt Marsh sink beneath the earth and flow underground, and to the south the source of the Yellow River emerges from them.

sentences.zh: 多玉石，河注中國。

translations.literal: There is much jade; the river flows into China.
translations.idiomatic: The region abounds in jade. The river flows onward into China.

sentences.zh: 而樓蘭、姑師邑有城郭，臨鹽澤。

translations.literal: Loulan and Gushi are towns with walled cities, situated beside the Salt Marsh.
translations.idiomatic: The towns of Loulan and Gushi are walled settlements situated on the shores of the Salt Marsh.

sentences.zh: 鹽澤去長安可五千里。

translations.literal: The Salt Marsh is approximately five thousand li from Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The Salt Marsh lies roughly five thousand li from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 匈奴右方居鹽澤以東，至隴西長城，南接羌，鬲漢道焉。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu right flank occupies the area east of the Salt Marsh to the Great Wall at Longxi, bordering the Qiang to the south, thus blocking the Han route.
translations.idiomatic: The right wing of the Xiongnu occupies the territory east of the Salt Marsh all the way to the Great Wall at Longxi, with the Qiang bordering them to the south. This effectively blocks the road from Han.

sentences.zh: 烏孫在大宛東北可二千里，行國，隨畜，與匈奴同俗。

translations.literal: The Wusun are about two thousand li northeast of Dayuan; they are a nomadic state, following their herds, with customs like the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun live roughly two thousand li northeast of Dayuan. They are a nomadic people who follow their herds from pasture to pasture, with customs similar to those of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 控弦者數萬，敢戰。

translations.literal: Those who draw the bow number tens of thousands; they are bold in battle.
translations.idiomatic: They can muster tens of thousands of mounted archers, and they are fierce in battle.

sentences.zh: 故服匈奴，及盛，取其羈屬，不肯往朝會焉。

translations.literal: Formerly they submitted to the Xiongnu, but when they grew powerful, they threw off Xiongnu suzerainty and refused to attend court gatherings.
translations.idiomatic: In earlier times they had been subjects of the Xiongnu, but as they grew in strength they cast off this vassalage and refused to attend the Xiongnu court assemblies.

sentences.zh: 康居在大宛西北可二千里，行國，與月氏大同俗。

translations.literal: Kangju lies about two thousand li northwest of Dayuan; it is a nomadic state with customs largely the same as the Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju lies roughly two thousand li northwest of Dayuan. It is a nomadic state whose customs closely resemble those of the Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 控弦者八九萬人。

translations.literal: Those who draw the bow number eighty to ninety thousand.
translations.idiomatic: They can muster eighty to ninety thousand mounted archers.

sentences.zh: 與大宛鄰國。

translations.literal: It borders Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The state borders Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 國小，南羈事月氏，東羈事匈奴。

translations.literal: The state is small; to the south it is loosely subject to the Yuezhi, and to the east loosely subject to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: It is a small state, nominally subject to the Yuezhi to the south and to the Xiongnu to the east.

sentences.zh: 奄蔡在康居西北可二千里，行國，與康居大同俗。

translations.literal: Yancai lies about two thousand li northwest of Kangju; it is a nomadic state with customs largely the same as Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: Yancai lies roughly two thousand li northwest of Kangju. It is a nomadic state with customs very similar to those of Kangju.

sentences.zh: 控弦者十餘萬。

translations.literal: Those who draw the bow number over one hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: They can field over a hundred thousand mounted archers.

sentences.zh: 臨大澤，無崖，蓋乃北海云。

translations.literal: They border a great lake without shores; it is presumably the Northern Sea.
translations.idiomatic: They border a vast body of water with no visible shore, which is said to be the Northern Sea.

sentences.zh: 大月氏在大宛西可二三千里，居媯水北。

translations.literal: The Great Yuezhi are about two to three thousand li west of Dayuan, dwelling north of the Gui River.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Yuezhi are situated some two to three thousand li west of Dayuan, dwelling north of the Oxus River.

sentences.zh: 其南則大夏，西則安息，北則康居。

translations.literal: To their south lies Daxia, to the west Anxi, and to the north Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: To the south lies Bactria, to the west Parthia, and to the north Kangju.

sentences.zh: 行國也，隨畜移徙，與匈奴同俗。

translations.literal: They are a nomadic state, migrating with their herds, with customs like the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: They are a nomadic people who move with their herds, following customs similar to those of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 控弦者可一二十萬。

translations.literal: Those who draw the bow number approximately one hundred to two hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: They can muster between one and two hundred thousand mounted archers.

sentences.zh: 故時彊，輕匈奴，及冒頓立，攻破月氏，至匈奴老上單于，殺月氏王，以其頭為飲器。

translations.literal: In former times they were powerful and looked down on the Xiongnu; but when Maodun rose, he attacked and defeated the Yuezhi, and by the time of the Laoshang Chanyu, the Yuezhi king was killed and his skull made into a drinking vessel.
translations.idiomatic: In former times the Yuezhi were powerful and held the Xiongnu in contempt. But after Maodun rose to power, he attacked and defeated the Yuezhi. Under the Laoshang Chanyu, the Yuezhi king was slain and his skull fashioned into a drinking vessel.

sentences.zh: 始月氏居敦煌、祁連閒，及為匈奴所敗，乃遠去，過宛，西擊大夏而臣之，遂都媯水北，為王庭。

translations.literal: Originally the Yuezhi dwelt between Dunhuang and the Qilian Mountains; when defeated by the Xiongnu, they moved far away, crossed Dayuan, attacked Daxia to the west and subjugated it, then established their capital north of the Gui River as the royal court.
translations.idiomatic: Originally, the Yuezhi had dwelt in the region between Dunhuang and the Qilian Mountains. After their defeat by the Xiongnu, they migrated far to the west, crossed through Dayuan, conquered Bactria and made it a vassal state, and established their royal court north of the Oxus River.

sentences.zh: 其餘小眾不能去者，保南山羌，號小月氏。

translations.literal: The remaining small group that could not leave took refuge with the Qiang in the Southern Mountains and were called the Lesser Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: A remnant too small to make the journey took refuge among the Qiang of the Southern Mountains and became known as the Lesser Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 安息在大月氏西可數千里。

translations.literal: Anxi lies several thousand li west of the Great Yuezhi.
translations.idiomatic: Parthia lies several thousand li to the west of the Great Yuezhi.

sentences.zh: 其俗土著，耕田，田稻麥，蒲陶酒。

translations.literal: Their custom is to be settled; they farm the land, cultivating rice and wheat, and make grape wine.
translations.idiomatic: The people are settled farmers who cultivate rice and wheat, and they produce grape wine.

sentences.zh: 城邑如大宛。

translations.literal: Their cities are like those of Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: Their cities and towns resemble those of Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 其屬小大數百城，地方數千里，最為大國。

translations.literal: Their subordinate cities, large and small, number several hundred; their territory extends several thousand li, making them the greatest state.
translations.idiomatic: They govern several hundred cities of various sizes, and their territory stretches across several thousand li, making Parthia the greatest state in the region.

sentences.zh: 臨媯水，有市，民商賈用車及船，行旁國或數千里。

translations.literal: It borders the Gui River, has markets; the people trade using carts and boats, traveling to neighboring states sometimes thousands of li.
translations.idiomatic: The country borders the Oxus River and has bustling markets. The merchants travel by cart and boat to neighboring states, sometimes covering distances of several thousand li.

sentences.zh: 以銀為錢，錢如其王面，王死輒更錢，效王面焉。

translations.literal: They use silver as money; the coins bear the face of their king; when the king dies they change the coins to bear the new king's face.
translations.idiomatic: They mint silver coins bearing the face of their king. When a king dies, the coins are recast with the likeness of his successor.

sentences.zh: 畫革旁行以為書記。

translations.literal: They write on leather in horizontal lines as their form of record-keeping.
translations.idiomatic: They write horizontally on leather for their records.

sentences.zh: 其西則條枝，北有奄蔡、黎軒。

translations.literal: To their west lies Tiaozhi; to the north are Yancai and Lijian.
translations.idiomatic: To the west lies Tiaozhi, and to the north are Yancai and Lijian.

sentences.zh: 條枝在安息西數千里，臨西海。

translations.literal: Tiaozhi lies several thousand li west of Anxi, bordering the Western Sea.
translations.idiomatic: Tiaozhi lies several thousand li west of Parthia, on the shore of the Western Sea.

sentences.zh: 暑溼。

translations.literal: It is hot and humid.
translations.idiomatic: The climate is hot and humid.

sentences.zh: 耕田，田稻。

translations.literal: They farm the land, cultivating rice.
translations.idiomatic: The people farm the land and cultivate rice.

sentences.zh: 有大鳥，卵如甕。

translations.literal: There are large birds whose eggs are as big as jars.
translations.idiomatic: There are great birds whose eggs are the size of earthen jars.

sentences.zh: 人眾甚多，往往有小君長，而安息役屬之，以為外國。

translations.literal: The population is very large; there are petty chiefs everywhere, but Anxi has subjugated them, treating them as an outer vassal.
translations.idiomatic: The population is very large, and there are petty chieftains scattered throughout, but Parthia has subjugated them all and treats the land as an outlying dependency.

sentences.zh: 國善眩。

translations.literal: The country excels at conjuring tricks.
translations.idiomatic: The people of this land are skilled in the art of conjuring.

sentences.zh: 安息長老傳聞條枝有弱水、西王母，而未嘗見。

translations.literal: The elders of Anxi have heard legends that Tiaozhi has the Weak Water and the Queen Mother of the West, but they have never seen them.
translations.idiomatic: The elders of Parthia speak of legends that beyond Tiaozhi lie the Weak Water and the Queen Mother of the West, though none has ever laid eyes on them.

sentences.zh: 大夏在大宛西南二千餘里媯水南。

translations.literal: Daxia lies more than two thousand li southwest of Dayuan, south of the Gui River.
translations.idiomatic: Bactria lies over two thousand li to the southwest of Dayuan, on the southern bank of the Oxus River.

sentences.zh: 其俗土著，有城屋，與大宛同俗。

translations.literal: Their custom is to be settled; they have walled cities and houses, with customs similar to Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The people are settled, with walled cities and houses, and their customs resemble those of Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 無大（王）［君］長，往往城邑置小長。

translations.literal: They have no great ruler; each city has its own petty chief.
translations.idiomatic: They have no paramount ruler. Instead, each city and town appoints its own petty chief.

sentences.zh: 其兵弱，畏戰。

translations.literal: Their soldiers are weak and afraid of battle.
translations.idiomatic: Their military is weak, and the people are averse to warfare.

sentences.zh: 善賈市。

translations.literal: They are good at trade.
translations.idiomatic: They are skilled in commerce.

sentences.zh: 及大月氏西徙，攻敗之，皆臣畜大夏。

translations.literal: When the Great Yuezhi migrated west, they attacked and defeated them, subjugating all of Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: When the Great Yuezhi migrated westward, they attacked and defeated Bactria, reducing the entire land to vassalage.

sentences.zh: 大夏民多，可百餘萬。

translations.literal: Daxia has a large population, approximately over one million.
translations.idiomatic: Bactria has a large population, numbering over a million.

sentences.zh: 其都曰藍市城，有市販賈諸物。

translations.literal: Their capital is called Lanshi City, which has markets where all manner of goods are bought and sold.
translations.idiomatic: Their capital is called Lanshi, a city with thriving markets where all manner of goods are traded.

sentences.zh: 其東南有身毒國。

translations.literal: To its southeast lies the kingdom of Shendu.
translations.idiomatic: To the southeast lies the kingdom of India.

sentences.zh: 騫曰：「臣在大夏時，見邛竹杖、蜀布。

translations.literal: Qian said: 'When your servant was in Daxia, I saw Qiong bamboo staffs and Shu cloth.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian reported, "When I was in Bactria, I saw bamboo staffs from Qiong and cloth from Shu.

sentences.zh: 問曰：『安得此？』

translations.literal: I asked: 'Where did you get these?'
translations.idiomatic: I asked, 'Where did you obtain these?'

sentences.zh: 大夏國人曰：『吾賈人往市之身毒。

translations.literal: The people of Daxia said: 'Our merchants go to buy them in Shendu.'
translations.idiomatic: The Bactrians replied, 'Our merchants travel to India to purchase them.'

sentences.zh: 身毒在大夏東南可數千里。

translations.literal: Shendu lies several thousand li southeast of Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: India lies several thousand li to the southeast of Bactria.

sentences.zh: 其俗土著，大與大夏同，而卑溼暑熱云。

translations.literal: Its customs are settled, largely the same as Daxia, but the land is low-lying, humid, and hot.
translations.idiomatic: The people are settled, with customs largely resembling those of Bactria, though the land is low-lying, humid, and hot.

sentences.zh: 其人民乘象以戰。

translations.literal: The people ride elephants in battle.
translations.idiomatic: The people ride elephants into battle.

sentences.zh: 其國臨大水焉。』

translations.literal: Their country borders a great river.'
translations.idiomatic: Their country borders a great river.'

sentences.zh: 以騫度之，大夏去漢萬二千里，居漢西南。

translations.literal: By Qian's estimation, Daxia lies twelve thousand li from Han, situated to Han's southwest.
translations.idiomatic: By Zhang Qian's reckoning, Bactria lies twelve thousand li from Han, to the southwest.

sentences.zh: 今身毒國又居大夏東南數千里，有蜀物，此其去蜀不遠矣。

translations.literal: Now India lies several thousand li southeast of Daxia and has Shu goods; therefore it cannot be far from Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Now, India lies several thousand li to the southeast of Bactria and possesses goods from Shu. It follows that India cannot be far from Shu.

sentences.zh: 今使大夏，從羌中，險，羌人惡之；

translations.literal: Now, to send envoys to Daxia through Qiang territory is dangerous; the Qiang are hostile;
translations.idiomatic: At present, sending envoys to Bactria through Qiang territory is perilous, for the Qiang are hostile.

sentences.zh: 少北，則為匈奴所得；

translations.literal: A little to the north, one falls into the hands of the Xiongnu;
translations.idiomatic: Veering even slightly to the north means falling into the hands of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 從蜀宜徑，又無寇。」

translations.literal: Going from Shu should be a direct route, and there would be no raiders.
translations.idiomatic: But a route from Shu should be more direct, and free from raiders."

sentences.zh: 天子既聞大宛及大夏、安息之屬皆大國，多奇物，土著，頗與中國同業，而兵弱，貴漢財物；

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven, having heard that Dayuan, Daxia, Anxi and such were all great states with many rare goods, settled peoples with industries similar to China's, but militarily weak, and that they valued Han goods;
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor, having heard that Dayuan, Bactria, Parthia, and their like were all great states, rich in rare goods, with settled populations and industries much like those of China, yet militarily weak and eager for Han merchandise;

sentences.zh: 其北有大月氏、康居之屬，兵彊，可以賂遺設利朝也。

translations.literal: that to their north were the Great Yuezhi, Kangju, and such, with strong armies, who could be won over to attend court through gifts and incentives.
translations.idiomatic: and that to the north the Great Yuezhi, Kangju, and others commanded powerful armies that might be won over as allies through gifts and inducements.

sentences.zh: 且誠得而以義屬之，則廣地萬里，重九譯，致殊俗，威德遍於四海。

translations.literal: Moreover, if these could truly be won over through righteousness, then the territory would expand ten thousand li, needing interpreters of nine languages, bringing exotic customs, and spreading the imperial majesty across all four seas.
translations.idiomatic: If these nations could truly be brought into the fold through righteous diplomacy, the Empire's reach would extend ten thousand li, drawing in peoples of many tongues and exotic customs, and spreading the imperial prestige to the four seas.

sentences.zh: 天子欣然，以騫言為然，乃令騫因蜀犍為發閒使，四道并出：出駹，出冉，出徙，出邛、僰，皆各行一二千里。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was delighted, agreed with Qian's proposal, and ordered Qian to dispatch covert envoys from Shu and Jianwei along four routes simultaneously: through Mang, through Ran, through Xi, and through Qiong and Bo, each traveling one to two thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor was delighted and accepted Zhang Qian's proposal. He ordered Zhang Qian to dispatch covert envoys from Shu and Jianwei along four simultaneous routes: through Mang, through Ran, through Xi, and through Qiong and Bo. Each party traveled one to two thousand li.

sentences.zh: 其北方閉氐、筰，南方閉巂、昆明。

translations.literal: To the north they were blocked by the Di and Zuo; to the south blocked by the Sui and Kunming.
translations.idiomatic: To the north they were blocked by the Di and Zuo peoples; to the south, by the Sui and Kunming.

sentences.zh: 昆明之屬無君長，善寇盜，輒殺略漢使，終莫得通。

translations.literal: The Kunming and their kind had no chiefs, were skilled at raiding and thieving, and always killed and plundered Han envoys, so the routes could never be opened.
translations.idiomatic: The Kunming and their kin had no paramount chiefs. They were notorious raiders and brigands who invariably killed and robbed the Han envoys, and in the end no route could be forced through.

sentences.zh: 然聞其西可千餘里有乘象國，名曰滇越，而蜀賈姦出物者或至焉，於是漢以求大夏道始通滇國。

translations.literal: However, it was heard that over a thousand li to the west there was a country that used elephants, named Dianyue, and that Shu merchants smuggling goods sometimes reached there; thus, in seeking the road to Daxia, Han first opened contact with the kingdom of Dian.
translations.idiomatic: Nevertheless, it was reported that over a thousand li to the west lay a land of elephant-riders called Dianyue, and that Shu merchants smuggling goods had sometimes reached it. In this way, Han's search for a route to Bactria first opened relations with the kingdom of Dian.

sentences.zh: 初，漢欲通西南夷，費多，道不通，罷之。

translations.literal: Earlier, Han had wished to open contact with the southwestern barbarians, but the cost was great, the road could not be opened, and the effort was abandoned.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, the Han court had attempted to establish contact with the southwestern tribes, but the expense was enormous and the routes impassable, so the effort was abandoned.

sentences.zh: 及張騫言可以通大夏，乃復事西南夷。

translations.literal: When Zhang Qian said that a route to Daxia could be opened, they renewed efforts with the southwestern barbarians.
translations.idiomatic: But when Zhang Qian argued that a route to Bactria could be opened this way, the court revived its efforts to reach the southwestern tribes.

sentences.zh: 騫以校尉從大將軍擊匈奴，知水草處，軍得以不乏，乃封騫為博望侯。

translations.literal: Qian served as a Colonel accompanying the Grand General to attack the Xiongnu; because he knew the locations of water and pasture, the army was kept from want, and so Qian was enfeoffed as Marquis of Bowang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian served as a Colonel under the Grand General in the campaign against the Xiongnu. His knowledge of where to find water and pasture kept the army well supplied, and for this he was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Bowang.

sentences.zh: 是歲元朔六年也。

translations.literal: This year was the sixth year of the Yuanshuo era.
translations.idiomatic: This was the sixth year of the Yuanshuo era.

sentences.zh: 其明年，騫為衛尉，與李將軍俱出右北平擊匈奴。

translations.literal: The following year, Qian served as Commandant of the Guards and marched out from Youbeiping with General Li to attack the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, Zhang Qian held the post of Commandant of the Guards and marched out from Youbeiping alongside General Li to attack the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 匈奴圍李將軍，軍失亡多；

translations.literal: The Xiongnu surrounded General Li, and the army suffered heavy losses and desertions;
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu encircled General Li's forces, and the army suffered heavy casualties and desertions.

sentences.zh: 而騫後期當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: Qian arrived late past the deadline and should have been executed, but he ransomed himself and was reduced to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian had failed to arrive by the appointed date, a crime punishable by death. He ransomed his life and was stripped of his title, reduced to the status of a commoner.

sentences.zh: 是歲漢遣驃騎破匈奴西（城）［域］數萬人，至祁連山。

translations.literal: That year Han dispatched the Swift Cavalry General who defeated tens of thousands of Xiongnu in the Western Regions, reaching the Qilian Mountains.
translations.idiomatic: That same year, the Han dispatched the General of Swift Cavalry, who defeated tens of thousands of Xiongnu in the Western Regions and advanced as far as the Qilian Mountains.

sentences.zh: 其明年，渾邪王率其民降漢，而金城、河西西并南山至鹽澤空無匈奴。

translations.literal: The following year, the Hunye King led his people to surrender to Han, and from Jincheng and the Hexi Corridor westward along the Southern Mountains to the Salt Marsh there were no more Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, the Hunye King led his people in surrender to the Han, and from Jincheng through the Hexi Corridor, westward along the Southern Mountains to the Salt Marsh, the land was emptied of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 匈奴時有候者到，而希矣。

translations.literal: Xiongnu scouts occasionally appeared, but they were rare.
translations.idiomatic: Xiongnu scouts still appeared from time to time, but they had become rare.

sentences.zh: 其後二年，漢擊走單于於幕北。

translations.literal: Two years later, Han attacked and drove the Chanyu north of the desert.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later, the Han drove the Chanyu beyond the northern desert.

sentences.zh: 是後天子數問騫大夏之屬。

translations.literal: After this, the Son of Heaven frequently questioned Qian about Daxia and related matters.
translations.idiomatic: After this, the Emperor repeatedly consulted Zhang Qian about Bactria and the other western states.

sentences.zh: 騫既失侯，因言曰：「臣居匈奴中，聞烏孫王號昆莫，昆莫之父，匈奴西邊小國也。

translations.literal: Qian, having lost his marquisate, took the opportunity to say: 'When your servant lived among the Xiongnu, I heard that the king of the Wusun is called Kunmo. Kunmo's father ruled a small state on the western frontier of the Xiongnu.'
translations.idiomatic: Having lost his marquisate, Zhang Qian seized the opportunity to speak: "When I lived among the Xiongnu, I heard that the king of the Wusun is called Kunmo. Kunmo's father had been the ruler of a small state on the western frontier of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 匈奴攻殺其父，而昆莫生棄於野。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu attacked and killed his father, and Kunmo as an infant was abandoned in the wilderness.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu attacked and killed his father, and the infant Kunmo was abandoned in the wilderness.

sentences.zh: 烏嗛肉蜚其上，狼往乳之。

translations.literal: A raven carried meat and flew above him, and a she-wolf came and suckled him.
translations.idiomatic: A raven carried meat and hovered over him, and a she-wolf came and nursed him.

sentences.zh: 單于怪以為神，而收長之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu marveled and considered him divine, and took him in and raised him.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu was astonished, believed the child was blessed by the spirits, and took him in to raise as his own.

sentences.zh: 及壯，使將兵，數有功，單于復以其父之民予昆莫，令長守於西（城）［域］。

translations.literal: When he grew up, he was given command of troops and won merit repeatedly; the Chanyu then restored his father's people to Kunmo and ordered him to guard the Western Regions.
translations.idiomatic: When Kunmo came of age, he was given command of troops and distinguished himself in battle on several occasions. The Chanyu then restored his father's people to him and charged him with guarding the Western Regions.

sentences.zh: 昆莫收養其民，攻旁小邑，控弦數萬，習攻戰。

translations.literal: Kunmo gathered and raised his people, attacked neighboring small states, and mustered tens of thousands of archers skilled in warfare.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo rallied his people, conquered the neighboring small states, and built up a force of tens of thousands of mounted archers well trained in the arts of war.

sentences.zh: 單于死，昆莫乃率其眾遠徙，中立，不肯朝會匈奴。

translations.literal: When the Chanyu died, Kunmo led his people to migrate far away, declared neutrality, and refused to attend Xiongnu court gatherings.
translations.idiomatic: When the Chanyu died, Kunmo led his people far away, declared his independence, and refused to attend the Xiongnu court assemblies.

sentences.zh: 匈奴遣奇兵擊，不勝，以為神而遠之，因羈屬之，不大攻。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu sent crack troops to attack him but could not prevail; they considered him divine and kept their distance, maintaining only nominal suzerainty and not launching major attacks.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu dispatched crack troops against him but could not prevail. Believing him to be under divine protection, they kept their distance, maintaining only nominal suzerainty and refraining from further major attacks.

sentences.zh: 今單于新困於漢，而故渾邪地空無人。

translations.literal: Now the Chanyu has recently been pressed hard by Han, and the former Hunye territory is empty and uninhabited.
translations.idiomatic: Now the Chanyu has recently been pressed hard by the Han, and the former territory of the Hunye King lies empty and abandoned.

sentences.zh: 蠻夷俗貪漢財物，今誠以此時而厚幣賂烏孫，招以益東，居故渾邪之地，與漢結昆弟，其勢宜聽，聽則是斷匈奴右臂也。

translations.literal: The barbarians by custom covet Han wealth; if we truly seize this moment to lavish gifts upon the Wusun, entice them to move east and settle in the former Hunye territory, and forge a brotherly alliance with Han, the circumstances favor their acceptance; if they accept, it would be like cutting off the right arm of the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The frontier peoples have always coveted the wealth of Han. If we seize this moment to lavish gifts upon the Wusun and entice them to move eastward into the former Hunye territory, forging a fraternal alliance with Han, the circumstances strongly favor their acceptance. And if they accept, it would be like severing the right arm of the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 既連烏孫，自其西大夏之屬皆可招來而為外臣。」

translations.literal: Once allied with the Wusun, all the states to their west, including Daxia and the like, can be drawn in as outer vassals.
translations.idiomatic: Once we are allied with the Wusun, all the states to their west, including Bactria and its neighbors, can be drawn in as outer vassals."

sentences.zh: 天子以為然，拜騫為中郎將，將三百人，馬各二匹，牛羊以萬數，齎金幣帛直數千巨萬，多持節副使，道可使，使遺之他旁國。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven agreed, appointed Qian as General of the Gentlemen of the Household, gave him three hundred men, two horses each, cattle and sheep numbering in the tens of thousands, gold, coins, and silk worth tens of billions, and numerous deputy envoys bearing tallies, so that along the route he could dispatch them as envoys to other neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor agreed and appointed Zhang Qian as General of the Gentlemen of the Household, placing three hundred men under his command, each with two horses. He was furnished with cattle and sheep numbering in the tens of thousands, along with gold, coins, and silk worth thousands of millions. Numerous deputy envoys bearing official tallies accompanied him, so that along the route they could be dispatched as envoys to neighboring states.

sentences.zh: 騫既至烏孫，烏孫王昆莫見漢使如單于禮，騫大慚，知蠻夷貪，乃曰：「天子致賜，王不拜則還賜。」

translations.literal: When Qian arrived at the Wusun, the Wusun king Kunmo received the Han envoy with the same protocol as for the Chanyu. Qian was greatly humiliated; knowing that the barbarians were covetous, he said: 'The Son of Heaven has bestowed these gifts; if the King does not bow, the gifts will be returned.'
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Qian arrived among the Wusun, King Kunmo received the Han envoy with the same protocol he used for the Chanyu. Zhang Qian was deeply humiliated. Knowing the frontier peoples' covetousness, he declared, "The Emperor has bestowed these gifts. If the King does not bow to receive them, the gifts will be taken back."

sentences.zh: 昆莫起拜賜，其他如故。

translations.literal: Kunmo rose and bowed to receive the gifts; in all other respects things remained as before.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo rose and bowed to accept the gifts, though in all other respects his manner remained unchanged.

sentences.zh: 騫諭使指曰：「烏孫能東居渾邪地，則漢遣翁主為昆莫夫人。」

translations.literal: Qian conveyed the purpose of his mission, saying: 'If the Wusun can move east and settle in the Hunye territory, then Han will send a princess to be Kunmo's wife.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian conveyed the purpose of his mission: "If the Wusun will move east and settle in the former Hunye territory, the Han will send a princess to be Kunmo's consort."

sentences.zh: 烏孫國分，王老，而遠漢，未知其大小，素服屬匈奴日久矣，且又近之，其大臣皆畏胡，不欲移徙，王不能專制。

translations.literal: The Wusun kingdom was divided; the king was old; they were far from Han and did not know its size; they had long been accustomed to submission to the Xiongnu and were moreover close to them; all the great ministers feared the Xiongnu and did not wish to relocate; the king could not act unilaterally.
translations.idiomatic: But the Wusun kingdom was divided, and the king was old. They were far from Han and had no sense of its size. Having been accustomed to Xiongnu overlordship for generations and living in close proximity to them, all the senior ministers feared the Xiongnu and were unwilling to relocate. The king could not act on his own authority.

sentences.zh: 騫不得其要領。

translations.literal: Qian could not obtain the agreement he sought.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian was unable to win their commitment.

sentences.zh: 昆莫有十餘子，其中子曰大祿，彊，善將眾，將眾別居萬餘騎。

translations.literal: Kunmo had more than ten sons; his middle son was named Dalu, who was powerful, skilled at commanding troops, and lived separately with his own force of over ten thousand cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo had over ten sons. His middle son, Dalu, was powerful and skilled in command. He lived apart with a personal force of over ten thousand cavalry.

sentences.zh: 大祿兄為太子，太子有子曰岑娶，而太子蚤死。

translations.literal: Dalu's elder brother was the Crown Prince; the Crown Prince had a son named Cenqu, but the Crown Prince died young.
translations.idiomatic: Dalu's elder brother had been the Crown Prince. The Crown Prince had a son named Cenqu, but the Crown Prince died young.

sentences.zh: 臨死謂其父昆莫曰：「必以岑娶為太子，無令他人代之。」

translations.literal: On his deathbed he said to his father Kunmo: 'You must make Cenqu the Crown Prince; do not let anyone else take his place.'
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed, the Crown Prince said to his father Kunmo, "You must make Cenqu the heir. Let no one else take his place."

sentences.zh: 昆莫哀而許之，卒以岑娶為太子。

translations.literal: Kunmo grieved and agreed, ultimately making Cenqu the Crown Prince.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo was grief-stricken and consented, ultimately naming Cenqu as the Crown Prince.

sentences.zh: 大祿怒其不得代太子也，乃收其諸昆弟，將其眾畔，謀攻岑娶及昆莫。

translations.literal: Dalu, enraged that he was not made Crown Prince, gathered his brothers, led his followers in revolt, and plotted to attack Cenqu and Kunmo.
translations.idiomatic: Dalu was furious at being passed over for the succession. He rallied his brothers, led his followers into open rebellion, and plotted to attack both Cenqu and Kunmo.

sentences.zh: 昆莫老，常恐大祿殺岑娶，予岑娶萬餘騎別居，而昆莫有萬餘騎自備，國眾分為三，而其大總取羈屬昆莫，昆莫亦以此不敢專約於騫。

translations.literal: Kunmo, being old, constantly feared Dalu would kill Cenqu; he gave Cenqu over ten thousand cavalry to live separately, while Kunmo kept over ten thousand cavalry for his own protection. The kingdom's forces were divided into three, though overall nominal allegiance belonged to Kunmo. Because of this, Kunmo did not dare make an exclusive agreement with Qian.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo, now old, lived in constant fear that Dalu would kill Cenqu. He gave Cenqu a separate force of over ten thousand cavalry and kept another ten thousand for his own protection. The kingdom was thus split into three factions, though all nominally owed allegiance to Kunmo. For this reason, Kunmo did not dare commit himself exclusively to Zhang Qian's proposal.

sentences.zh: 騫因分遣副使使大宛、康居、大月氏、大夏、安息、身毒、于窴、扜穼及諸旁國。

translations.literal: Qian accordingly dispatched deputy envoys to Dayuan, Kangju, the Great Yuezhi, Daxia, Anxi, Shendu, Khotan, Yumi, and various neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Qian accordingly dispatched his deputy envoys to Dayuan, Kangju, the Great Yuezhi, Bactria, Parthia, India, Khotan, Yumi, and various other neighboring states.

sentences.zh: 烏孫發導譯送騫還，騫與烏孫遣使數十人，馬數十匹報謝，因令窺漢，知其廣大。

translations.literal: The Wusun sent guides and interpreters to escort Qian home. Qian brought back Wusun envoys numbering several dozen, with several dozen horses as gifts of thanks, so they could observe Han and learn of its vastness.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun provided guides and interpreters to escort Zhang Qian home. Accompanying him were several dozen Wusun envoys with as many horses, sent to offer thanks and to observe the Han Empire firsthand and appreciate its vastness.

sentences.zh: 騫還到，拜為大行，列於九卿。

translations.literal: When Qian returned, he was appointed Director of Foreign Relations, ranking among the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Upon his return, Zhang Qian was appointed Director of Foreign Relations, a post ranked among the Nine Ministers.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，卒。

translations.literal: A little over a year later, he died.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later, he died.

sentences.zh: 烏孫使既見漢人眾富厚，歸報其國，其國乃益重漢。

translations.literal: When the Wusun envoys saw the vast population and great wealth of Han, they reported back to their country, and their country thereafter held Han in much greater esteem.
translations.idiomatic: When the Wusun envoys saw the vast population and great wealth of the Han Empire, they returned and reported to their own court, after which the Wusun held Han in considerably higher regard.

sentences.zh: 其後歲餘，騫所遣使通大夏之屬者皆頗與其人俱來，於是西北國始通於漢矣。

translations.literal: Over a year later, the deputy envoys that Qian had dispatched to Daxia and other states all returned, bringing foreign envoys with them. From this point, the states of the northwest first opened relations with Han.
translations.idiomatic: Over a year later, the deputy envoys Zhang Qian had dispatched to Bactria and the other states all returned, bringing foreign envoys back with them. From this point on, the states of the northwest first established diplomatic relations with Han.

sentences.zh: 然張騫鑿空，其後使往者皆稱博望侯，以為質於外國，外國由此信之。

translations.literal: Thus Zhang Qian blazed the trail into the unknown; all envoys who went thereafter invoked the name of the Marquis of Bowang as a guarantee to foreign states, and by this the foreign states trusted them.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Zhang Qian blazed the trail into the unknown. All envoys who followed invoked the name of the Marquis of Bowang as a pledge of good faith to the foreign states, and through his reputation those states came to trust them.

sentences.zh: 自博望侯騫死後，匈奴聞漢通烏孫，怒，欲擊之。

translations.literal: After the Marquis of Bowang died, the Xiongnu heard that Han had opened relations with the Wusun, were angered, and wished to attack them.
translations.idiomatic: After the death of the Marquis of Bowang, the Xiongnu learned that the Han had established relations with the Wusun. Enraged, they planned to attack the Wusun.

sentences.zh: 及漢使烏孫，若出其南，抵大宛、大月氏相屬，烏孫乃恐，使使獻馬，願得尚漢女翁主為昆弟。

translations.literal: Moreover, when Han envoys to the Wusun traveled south to reach Dayuan, the Great Yuezhi, and their neighbors, the Wusun became alarmed, sent envoys with horse tributes, and requested to marry a Han princess to establish a fraternal alliance.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, as Han envoys to the Wusun also traveled south to Dayuan, the Great Yuezhi, and their neighbors in succession, the Wusun grew alarmed. They dispatched envoys bearing tribute horses and requested the hand of a Han princess to seal a fraternal alliance.

sentences.zh: 天子問群臣議計，皆曰「必先納聘，然後乃遣女」。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven consulted his ministers for advice; all said: 'The bride-price must first be received, then the princess may be sent.'
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor consulted his ministers, who all agreed: "The bride-price must be received first, and only then should the princess be sent."

sentences.zh: 初，天子發書易，云「神馬當從西北來」。

translations.literal: Earlier, the Son of Heaven had consulted the Book of Changes, which said: 'Divine horses shall come from the northwest.'
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, the Emperor had consulted the Book of Changes, which foretold: "Divine horses shall come from the northwest."

sentences.zh: 得烏孫馬好，名曰「天馬」。

translations.literal: When the fine horses from the Wusun were obtained, they were named 'Heavenly Horses.'
translations.idiomatic: When the fine horses of the Wusun arrived, the Emperor named them the "Heavenly Horses."

sentences.zh: 及得大宛汗血馬，益壯，更名烏孫馬曰「西極」，名大宛馬曰「天馬」云。

translations.literal: When the blood-sweating horses of Dayuan were later obtained, being even more magnificent, the Wusun horses were renamed 'Western Extremity' and the Dayuan horses were called 'Heavenly Horses.'
translations.idiomatic: When the blood-sweating horses of Dayuan were later obtained and proved even more magnificent, the Wusun horses were downgraded to "Horses of the Western Extremity," and the Dayuan horses were given the title "Heavenly Horses."

sentences.zh: 而漢始筑令居以西，初置酒泉郡以通西北國。

translations.literal: Han then began building west of Lingju and first established Jiuquan Commandery to open communications with the northwestern states.
translations.idiomatic: The Han then began construction west of Lingju and established the Jiuquan Commandery for the first time, to open a corridor to the northwestern states.

sentences.zh: 因益發使抵安息、奄蔡、黎軒、條枝、身毒國。

translations.literal: Consequently, even more envoys were dispatched to Anxi, Yancai, Lijian, Tiaozhi, and Shendu.
translations.idiomatic: Accordingly, even more envoys were dispatched to Parthia, Yancai, Lijian, Tiaozhi, and India.

sentences.zh: 而天子好宛馬，使者相望於道。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was fond of the horses of Dayuan, and envoys could be seen one after another on the road.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor was enamored of the horses of Dayuan, and the envoys dispatched to obtain them were so numerous that each could see the next on the road ahead.

sentences.zh: 諸使外國一輩大者數百，少者百餘人，人所齎操大放博望侯時。

translations.literal: Each embassy to foreign states numbered at most several hundred and at least over a hundred people; the gifts each person carried were modeled on those of the Marquis of Bowang's time.
translations.idiomatic: Each embassy to the foreign states numbered between one and several hundred men, and the gifts they carried were modeled on those of the Marquis of Bowang's missions.

sentences.zh: 其后益習而衰少焉。

translations.literal: Later, as these became routine, the scale gradually diminished.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as such missions became routine, the scale gradually diminished.

sentences.zh: 漢率一歲中使多者十餘，少者五六輩，遠者八九歲，近者數歲而反。

translations.literal: Han on average dispatched over ten embassies a year at most, and five or six at least; the farthest journeys took eight or nine years, the nearest several years to return.
translations.idiomatic: On average, the Han dispatched as many as ten or more embassies a year, and at least five or six. The most distant missions took eight or nine years to return, and even the nearer ones took several years.

sentences.zh: 是時漢既滅越，而蜀、西南夷皆震，請吏入朝。

translations.literal: At this time, Han had already destroyed Yue, and Shu and the southwestern barbarians were all alarmed and requested officials to attend court.
translations.idiomatic: At this time, the Han had already destroyed the kingdoms of Yue. The peoples of Shu and the southwestern tribes were all shaken, and they sent envoys to attend the imperial court.

sentences.zh: 於是置益州、越巂、牂柯、沈黎、汶山郡，欲地接以前通大夏。

translations.literal: Thereupon the commanderies of Yizhou, Yuesui, Zangke, Shenli, and Wenshan were established, intending to link up the territory and open the way to Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: The commanderies of Yizhou, Yuesui, Zangke, Shenli, and Wenshan were established, with the aim of linking up the territory and opening a route to Bactria.

sentences.zh: 乃遣使柏始昌、呂越人等歲十餘輩，出此初郡抵大夏，皆復閉昆明，為所殺，奪幣財，終莫能通至大夏焉。

translations.literal: So they dispatched envoys such as Bo Shichang and Lu Yueren, over ten groups a year, setting out from these new commanderies toward Daxia; but all were again blocked by the Kunming, who killed them and seized their gifts, so that in the end none could get through to Daxia.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys such as Bo Shichang and Lu Yueren were dispatched, over ten groups a year, setting out from these new commanderies toward Bactria. But all were blocked by the Kunming, who killed them and seized their gifts. In the end, none could get through to Bactria.

sentences.zh: 於是漢發三輔罪人，因巴蜀士數萬人，遣兩將軍郭昌、衛廣等往擊昆明之遮漢使者，斬首虜數萬人而去。

translations.literal: Thereupon Han mobilized convicts from the Three Adjuncts and tens of thousands of soldiers from Ba and Shu, dispatching Generals Guo Chang and Wei Guang to attack the Kunming who had been intercepting Han envoys; they beheaded and captured tens of thousands before withdrawing.
translations.idiomatic: The Han then mobilized convicts from the Three Adjuncts and tens of thousands of troops from Ba and Shu, dispatching Generals Guo Chang and Wei Guang to attack the Kunming who had been waylaying Han envoys. They killed or captured tens of thousands before withdrawing.

sentences.zh: 其後遣使，昆明復為寇，竟莫能得通。

translations.literal: When envoys were sent later, the Kunming resumed their raiding, and in the end the route could not be opened.
translations.idiomatic: When envoys were sent again afterward, the Kunming resumed their raiding, and in the end the route still could not be forced through.

sentences.zh: 而北道酒泉抵大夏，使者既多，而外國益厭漢幣，不貴其物。

translations.literal: Meanwhile, on the northern route from Jiuquan to Daxia, there were already many envoys, and the foreign states grew weary of Han currency and no longer valued their goods.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile, on the northern route from Jiuquan to Bactria, envoys had become so numerous that the foreign states grew weary of Han gifts and no longer prized their goods.

sentences.zh: 自博望侯開外國道以尊貴，其後從吏卒皆爭上書言外國奇怪利害，求使。

translations.literal: Ever since the Marquis of Bowang opened the road to foreign states and won honor and high rank, attendants and soldiers afterward all competed to submit memorials about the wonders and opportunities of foreign lands, seeking to be appointed as envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since the Marquis of Bowang had opened the road to the foreign states and won great honor, attendants and soldiers all scrambled to submit memorials describing the wonders and opportunities of foreign lands, seeking appointment as envoys.

sentences.zh: 天子為其絕遠，非人所樂往，聽其言，予節，募吏民毋問所從來，為具備人眾遣之，以廣其道。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven, because the destinations were extremely remote and not places people willingly went, listened to their proposals, granted them tallies, recruited officials and commoners regardless of background, outfitted them with men and provisions, and sent them off to broaden the routes.
translations.idiomatic: Because the destinations were so remote and no one went willingly, the Emperor accepted their proposals, granted them envoy's tallies, and recruited officials and commoners of any background, outfitting them with men and provisions before sending them on their way to expand the network of routes.

sentences.zh: 來還不能毋侵盜幣物，及使失指，天子為其習之，輒覆案致重罪，以激怒令贖，復求使。

translations.literal: Upon returning, they invariably embezzled gifts and goods, or failed in their mission's objectives; the Son of Heaven, being accustomed to this, would investigate and charge them with serious crimes, provoking them to ransom themselves, after which they would seek another envoy appointment.
translations.idiomatic: Upon returning, these envoys invariably proved to have embezzled gifts and goods or to have failed in their mission's purpose. The Emperor, accustomed to such behavior, would order investigations and press serious charges, provoking the offenders to ransom themselves. Afterward, they would simply seek another envoy appointment.

sentences.zh: 使端無窮，而輕犯法。

translations.literal: The pretexts for envoy missions were endless, and people thought little of breaking the law.
translations.idiomatic: The pretexts for new missions were inexhaustible, and the envoys thought nothing of breaking the law.

sentences.zh: 其吏卒亦輒復盛推外國所有，言大者予節，言小者為副，故妄言無行之徒皆爭效之。

translations.literal: The officials and soldiers also enthusiastically promoted what foreign states had to offer; those who made grand claims were given tallies, and those who made lesser claims were made deputies, so that all manner of boasters and scoundrels competed to follow their example.
translations.idiomatic: Officials and soldiers, for their part, eagerly promoted the wonders of the foreign states. Those who made the grandest claims were granted envoy tallies, and those who made lesser claims were appointed as deputies. In this way, all manner of boasters and scoundrels competed to follow their example.

sentences.zh: 其使皆貧人子，私縣官齎物，欲賤市以私其利外國。

translations.literal: These envoys were all sons of poor families who privately embezzled government-supplied goods, wishing to sell them cheaply in foreign lands for personal profit.
translations.idiomatic: The envoys were generally men of humble origins who embezzled the goods supplied by the government, intending to sell them cheaply in foreign lands and pocket the profit.

sentences.zh: 外國亦厭漢使人人有言輕重，度漢兵遠不能至，而禁其食物以苦漢使。

translations.literal: The foreign states also grew weary of Han envoys each making different and contradictory claims; reckoning that Han armies were too far away to reach them, they withheld food and provisions to torment the Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: The foreign states, for their part, grew weary of the Han envoys, each of whom made different and contradictory claims. Reckoning that the Han armies were too far away to pose any real threat, they withheld food and provisions to make life miserable for the Han envoys.

sentences.zh: 漢使乏絕積怨，至相攻擊。

translations.literal: The Han envoys, deprived of supplies and accumulating resentments, went so far as to attack each other.
translations.idiomatic: Deprived of supplies and seething with accumulated grievances, the Han envoys resorted to attacking one another.

sentences.zh: 而樓蘭、姑師小國耳，當空道，攻劫漢使王恢等尤甚。

translations.literal: Loulan and Gushi were merely small states, but they sat astride the empty road and attacked and robbed Han envoys such as Wang Hui with particular severity.
translations.idiomatic: Loulan and Gushi were mere petty states, but they sat astride the desert route and were especially aggressive in attacking and robbing Han envoys, with Wang Hui among those who suffered most.

sentences.zh: 而匈奴奇兵時時遮擊使西國者。

translations.literal: Moreover, Xiongnu raiding parties from time to time intercepted and attacked envoys heading to the western states.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile, Xiongnu raiding parties regularly intercepted and attacked envoys bound for the western states.

sentences.zh: 使者爭遍言外國災害，皆有城邑，兵弱易擊。

translations.literal: The envoys all competed to report that the foreign states were troublesome, but that they all had walled cities, their armies were weak, and they would be easy to conquer.
translations.idiomatic: The returning envoys all competed to report that the foreign states were troublesome but that they all had walled cities, their armies were weak, and they would be easy to conquer.

sentences.zh: 於是天子以故遣從驃侯破奴將屬國騎及郡兵數萬，至匈河水，欲以擊胡，胡皆去。

translations.literal: Therefore the Son of Heaven dispatched the Marquis of Congpiao, Zhao Ponu, leading cavalry from the dependent states and commandery troops numbering tens of thousands, to the Xionghe River, intending to attack the barbarians; but the barbarians all withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor accordingly dispatched Zhao Ponu, the Marquis of Congpiao, at the head of cavalry from the dependent states and commandery troops numbering tens of thousands, to the Xionghe River with the intention of attacking the Xiongnu. But the Xiongnu withdrew before battle.

sentences.zh: 其明年，擊姑師，破奴與輕騎七百餘先至，虜樓蘭王，遂破姑師。

translations.literal: The following year, in the attack on Gushi, Ponu arrived first with over seven hundred light cavalry, captured the King of Loulan, and subsequently destroyed Gushi.
translations.idiomatic: The following year, in the campaign against Gushi, Zhao Ponu rode ahead with over seven hundred light cavalry, captured the King of Loulan, and went on to crush Gushi.

sentences.zh: 因舉兵威以困烏孫、大宛之屬。

translations.literal: He then used the power of his army to intimidate the Wusun, Dayuan, and their neighbors.
translations.idiomatic: He then used the momentum of his victory to cow the Wusun, Dayuan, and the surrounding states into submission.

sentences.zh: 還，封破奴為浞野侯。

translations.literal: Upon his return, Ponu was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Zhuoye.
translations.idiomatic: Upon his return, Zhao Ponu was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Zhuoye.

sentences.zh: 王恢數使，為樓蘭所苦，言天子，天子發兵令恢佐破奴擊破之，封恢為浩侯。

translations.literal: Wang Hui, who had been on numerous missions and had suffered greatly at the hands of Loulan, reported to the Son of Heaven. The Son of Heaven dispatched troops and ordered Hui to assist Ponu in crushing them; Hui was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Hao.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hui, who had suffered greatly at the hands of Loulan on his many missions, petitioned the Emperor. The Emperor dispatched troops and ordered Hui to assist Zhao Ponu in crushing Loulan. For his role, Wang Hui was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Hao.

sentences.zh: 於是酒泉列亭鄣至玉門矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon a chain of watchtowers and barriers was erected from Jiuquan to the Jade Gate.
translations.idiomatic: A chain of watchtowers and barriers was then erected stretching from Jiuquan all the way to the Jade Gate.

sentences.zh: 烏孫以千匹馬聘漢女，漢遣宗室女江都翁主往妻烏孫，烏孫王昆莫以為右夫人。

translations.literal: The Wusun offered a thousand horses as bride-price for a Han woman. Han dispatched the Princess of Jiangdu, a daughter of the imperial clan, to marry the Wusun. King Kunmo made her his right consort.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun offered a thousand horses as bride-price. The Han court sent the Princess of Jiangdu, a daughter of the imperial clan, to marry among the Wusun. King Kunmo made her his right consort.

sentences.zh: 匈奴亦遣女妻昆莫，昆莫以為左夫人。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu also sent a woman to marry Kunmo; Kunmo made her his left consort.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu likewise sent a woman to wed Kunmo, and he made her his left consort.

sentences.zh: 昆莫曰「我老」，乃令其孫岑娶妻翁主。

translations.literal: Kunmo said 'I am old,' and had his grandson Cenqu marry the princess.
translations.idiomatic: Kunmo declared, "I am old," and had his grandson Cenqu marry the princess in his stead.

sentences.zh: 烏孫多馬，其富人至有四五千匹馬。

translations.literal: The Wusun have many horses; their wealthy families possess as many as four to five thousand horses.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun possess great numbers of horses. Their wealthiest families own as many as four to five thousand head.

sentences.zh: 初，漢使至安息，安息王令將二萬騎迎於東界。

translations.literal: Earlier, when the Han envoy reached Anxi, the King of Anxi ordered twenty thousand cavalry to greet them at the eastern border.
translations.idiomatic: When the first Han envoy reached Parthia, the Parthian king dispatched a cavalry escort of twenty thousand to meet them at the eastern frontier.

sentences.zh: 東界去王都數千里。

translations.literal: The eastern border was several thousand li from the royal capital.
translations.idiomatic: The eastern frontier lay several thousand li from the royal capital.

sentences.zh: 行比至，過數十城，人民相屬甚多。

translations.literal: Traveling until they arrived, they passed through scores of cities, with populous settlements stretching continuously.
translations.idiomatic: Along the way they passed through scores of cities, with populous settlements stretching in an unbroken chain.

sentences.zh: 漢使還，而後發使隨漢使來觀漢廣大，以大鳥卵及黎軒善眩人獻于漢。

translations.literal: When the Han envoy returned, the Parthians afterward sent envoys to accompany the Han envoy back to observe the vastness of Han, presenting ostrich eggs and skilled conjurers from Lijian as tribute.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han envoy departed, Parthia dispatched its own envoys to accompany him back to observe the vastness of the Han Empire. They presented ostrich eggs and skilled conjurers from Lijian as tribute to the Han court.

sentences.zh: 及宛西小國驩潛、大益，宛東姑師、扜穼、蘇薤之屬，皆隨漢使獻見天子。

translations.literal: Also, the small states west of Dayuan, such as Huanqian and Dayi, and those east of Dayuan, such as Gushi, Yumi, and Suxie, all sent envoys along with the Han envoys to present tribute to the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The small states west of Dayuan, such as Huanqian and Dayi, and those to the east, including Gushi, Yumi, and Suxie, all sent envoys to accompany the Han ambassadors and present tribute to the Emperor.

sentences.zh: 天子大悅。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor was greatly pleased.

sentences.zh: 而漢使窮河源，河源出于窴，其山多玉石，采來，天子案古圖書，名河所出山曰崑崙云。

translations.literal: Han envoys traced the Yellow River to its source; the source emerged from Khotan, and its mountains contained much jade, which they gathered and brought back. The Son of Heaven consulted ancient maps and named the mountain from which the river emerged as Kunlun.
translations.idiomatic: Han envoys traced the Yellow River to its source and found that it emerged from the mountains of Khotan, which were rich in jade. They gathered specimens and brought them back. The Emperor consulted the ancient maps and texts and proclaimed the mountain from which the river issued to be Kunlun.

sentences.zh: 是時上方數巡狩海上，乃悉從外國客，大都多人則過之，散財帛以賞賜，厚具以饒給之，以覽示漢富厚焉。

translations.literal: At this time, the Emperor was frequently touring the coast. He took all the foreign guests along, and wherever the cities were large and populous he paraded them through, distributing wealth and silks as gifts, lavishly provisioning them, in order to display the wealth and abundance of Han.
translations.idiomatic: At this time, the Emperor was making frequent tours along the coast. He brought all the foreign guests in his entourage, parading through the largest and most populous cities, lavishing them with gifts of wealth and silk and generous provisions, all to display the magnificence of the Han Empire.

sentences.zh: 於是大觳抵，出奇戲諸怪物，多聚觀者，行賞賜，酒池肉林，令外國客遍觀（名）［各］倉庫府藏之積，見漢之廣大，傾駭之。

translations.literal: Thereupon grand wrestling matches were held, along with acrobatic shows and strange spectacles, drawing great crowds of spectators. Rewards were distributed, with pools of wine and forests of meat. The foreign guests were shown all the storehouses and treasury reserves, so they could see the vastness of Han, which awed and astonished them.
translations.idiomatic: Grand wrestling matches were staged, along with acrobatic displays and exotic spectacles, drawing vast crowds. Rewards were lavished, with pools of wine and forests of meat. The foreign guests were given a tour of the storehouses and treasury reserves so they could witness firsthand the wealth of the Han Empire, which left them awed and astonished.

sentences.zh: 及加其眩者之工，而觳抵奇戲歲增變，甚盛益興，自此始。

translations.literal: With the addition of the conjurers' skills, the wrestling matches and exotic performances grew more elaborate each year, flourishing greatly; it all began from this time.
translations.idiomatic: The conjurers added their art to the spectacles, and the wrestling matches and exotic performances grew more elaborate with each passing year, flourishing ever more grandly. It all began from this time.

sentences.zh: 西北外國使，更來更去。

translations.literal: Envoys from the northwestern foreign states came and went in continuous succession.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys from the northwestern states came and went in a constant stream.

sentences.zh: 宛以西，皆自以遠，尚驕恣晏然，未可詘以禮羈縻而使也。

translations.literal: From Dayuan westward, all considered themselves far away, remained arrogant and complacent, and could not yet be brought into submission through diplomacy and courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: From Dayuan westward, all the states considered themselves remote and remained arrogant and complacent. They could not yet be brought to heel through diplomacy and courtesy alone.

sentences.zh: 自烏孫以西至安息，以近匈奴，匈奴困月氏也，匈奴使持單于一信，則國國傳送食，不敢留苦；

translations.literal: From the Wusun westward to Anxi, because of their proximity to the Xiongnu, who had crushed the Yuezhi, a Xiongnu envoy bearing a single letter from the Chanyu would be passed from state to state with food and provisions, none daring to detain or mistreat him;
translations.idiomatic: From the Wusun westward to Parthia, because these states lay near the Xiongnu, who had crushed the Yuezhi, a single Xiongnu envoy bearing a letter from the Chanyu would be passed from state to state with food and provisions along the way, and none dared detain or mistreat him.

sentences.zh: 及至漢使，非出幣帛不得食，不市畜不得騎用。

translations.literal: But when it came to Han envoys, without producing coins and silk they could not get food, and without purchasing livestock they could not get riding animals.
translations.idiomatic: But Han envoys received nothing unless they paid: without offering coins and silk they could not obtain food, and without purchasing animals they could not secure mounts.

sentences.zh: 所以然者，遠漢，而漢多財物，故必市乃得所欲，然以畏匈奴於漢使焉。

translations.literal: The reason was that they were far from Han, and Han had many goods, so they insisted on trade to get what they wanted, yet they feared the Xiongnu more than the Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: This was because they were far from Han and, though Han was rich in goods, they insisted on trade to obtain what they wanted. Moreover, they feared the Xiongnu far more than they respected the Han envoys.

sentences.zh: 宛左右以蒲陶為酒，富人藏酒至萬餘石，久者數十歲不敗。

translations.literal: In and around Dayuan, they make wine from grapes. The wealthy store wine up to ten thousand shi or more, with some vintages lasting decades without spoiling.
translations.idiomatic: In and around Dayuan, grape wine is produced in abundance. The wealthy store wine numbering over ten thousand shi, and the finest vintages keep for decades without spoiling.

sentences.zh: 俗嗜酒，馬嗜苜蓿。

translations.literal: The people are fond of wine, and the horses are fond of alfalfa.
translations.idiomatic: The people are fond of wine, and the horses feed on alfalfa.

sentences.zh: 漢使取其實來，於是天子始種苜蓿、蒲陶肥饒地。

translations.literal: Han envoys brought back their seeds, and thereupon the Son of Heaven first planted alfalfa and grapes on fertile land.
translations.idiomatic: The Han envoys brought seeds back with them, and the Emperor ordered alfalfa and grapes to be planted for the first time on the fertile lands of the capital region.

sentences.zh: 及天馬多，外國使來眾，則離宮別觀旁盡種蒲萄、苜蓿極望。

translations.literal: When the Heavenly Horses became numerous and foreign envoys came in great numbers, grapevines and alfalfa were planted around the detached palaces and lodges as far as the eye could see.
translations.idiomatic: As the Heavenly Horses multiplied and foreign envoys arrived in ever greater numbers, grapevines and alfalfa were planted around the detached palaces and lodges, stretching as far as the eye could see.

sentences.zh: 自大宛以西至安息，國雖頗異言，然大同俗，相知言。

translations.literal: From Dayuan westward to Anxi, although the states differ somewhat in language, their customs are largely the same and they can understand each other's speech.
translations.idiomatic: From Dayuan westward to Parthia, although the states speak somewhat different languages, their customs are broadly similar and they can make themselves understood to one another.

sentences.zh: 其人皆深眼，多須髯，善市賈，爭分銖。

translations.literal: The people all have deep-set eyes, thick beards, are skilled in commerce, and haggle over the smallest sums.
translations.idiomatic: The people all have deep-set eyes and thick beards. They are shrewd in commerce and haggle over the smallest coin.

sentences.zh: 俗貴女子，女子所言而丈夫乃決正。

translations.literal: The custom is to esteem women; women speak and husbands then decide accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Their custom holds women in high regard. The men make their decisions based on what the women say.

sentences.zh: 其地皆無絲漆，不知鑄錢器。

translations.literal: Their lands all lack silk and lacquer, and they do not know how to cast coins or metal vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Their lands produce neither silk nor lacquer, and they do not know the arts of casting coins or metal vessels.

sentences.zh: 及漢使亡卒降，教鑄作他兵器。

translations.literal: When Han deserters and surrendered soldiers arrived, they taught them to cast and make weapons and other implements.
translations.idiomatic: When Han deserters and surrendered soldiers arrived among them, they taught the local people how to cast weapons and other metal implements.

sentences.zh: 得漢黃白金，輒以為器，不用為幣。

translations.literal: When they obtained Han gold and silver, they always made them into vessels, not using them as currency.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever they obtained Han gold and silver, they fashioned them into vessels rather than using them as currency.

sentences.zh: 而漢使者往既多，其少從率多進熟於天子，言曰：「宛有善馬在貳師城，匿不肯與漢使。」

translations.literal: As Han envoys went in great numbers, their younger attendants often ingratiated themselves with the Son of Heaven by reporting: 'Dayuan has fine horses in the city of Ershi, but they conceal them and refuse to give them to Han envoys.'
translations.idiomatic: As Han envoys traveled west in great numbers, their junior attendants often sought to ingratiate themselves with the Emperor by reporting, "Dayuan has superb horses in the city of Ershi, but they hide them and refuse to hand them over to Han envoys."

sentences.zh: 天子既好宛馬，聞之甘心，使壯士車令等持千金及金馬以請宛王貳師城善馬。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven, already fond of Dayuan horses, was thrilled to hear this. He sent the strong man Che Ling and others bearing a thousand catties of gold and a golden horse statue to request the fine horses of Ershi City from the King of Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor, already enamored of the horses of Dayuan, was eager to act. He dispatched the warrior Che Ling and others, bearing a thousand catties of gold and a golden horse statue, to request the fine horses of Ershi from the King of Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 宛國饒漢物，相與謀曰：「漢去我遠，而鹽水中數敗，出其北有胡寇，出其南乏水草。

translations.literal: The state of Dayuan, already rich in Han goods, deliberated among themselves: 'Han is far from us; crossing the Salt Desert they have often come to grief; to the north there are Xiongnu raiders, and to the south there is a lack of water and pasture.'
translations.idiomatic: The court of Dayuan, already well supplied with Han goods, deliberated among themselves: "Han is far from us. Their caravans have repeatedly come to grief crossing the Salt Desert. To the north lie Xiongnu raiders, and to the south there is neither water nor pasture.

sentences.zh: 又且往往而絕邑，乏食者多。

translations.literal: Moreover, there are many stretches without settlements, and many who lack food.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, there are long stretches without any settlements, and many travelers perish for want of food.

sentences.zh: 漢使數百人為輩來，而常乏食，死者過半，是安能致大軍乎？

translations.literal: Han envoys come in groups of several hundred, yet they constantly lack food and more than half die. How then could they bring a great army?
translations.idiomatic: When Han envoys come in parties of several hundred, they constantly run short of food and more than half of them perish. How could they possibly bring a great army against us?

sentences.zh: 無柰我何。

translations.literal: They can do nothing to us.
translations.idiomatic: There is nothing they can do to us.

sentences.zh: 且貳師馬，宛寶馬也。」

translations.literal: Moreover, the Ershi horses are Dayuan's treasured horses.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Ershi horses are the pride of Dayuan."

sentences.zh: 遂不肯予漢使。

translations.literal: Thus they refused to give them to the Han envoy.
translations.idiomatic: And so they refused to hand the horses over to the Han envoy.

sentences.zh: 漢使怒，妄言，椎金馬而去。

translations.literal: The Han envoy was furious, cursed them recklessly, smashed the golden horse, and departed.
translations.idiomatic: The Han envoy flew into a rage, hurled insults, smashed the golden horse statue, and stormed off.

sentences.zh: 宛貴人怒曰：「漢使至輕我！」

translations.literal: The Dayuan nobles were furious, saying: 'The Han envoy utterly despises us!'
translations.idiomatic: The nobles of Dayuan were outraged, saying, "This Han envoy shows us utter contempt!"

sentences.zh: 遣漢使去，令其東邊郁成遮攻殺漢使，取其財物。

translations.literal: They let the Han envoy leave, then ordered Yucheng on their eastern border to intercept, attack, and kill the Han envoy and seize his goods.
translations.idiomatic: They let the Han envoy depart, then ordered the garrison at Yucheng on their eastern frontier to intercept and kill him and seize his goods.

sentences.zh: 於是天子大怒。

translations.literal: At this, the Son of Heaven was greatly enraged.
translations.idiomatic: When word reached the capital, the Emperor was enraged.

sentences.zh: 諸嘗使宛姚定漢等言宛兵弱，誠以漢兵不過三千人，彊弩射之，即盡虜破宛矣。

translations.literal: Those who had previously been envoys to Dayuan, such as Yao Dinghan, said that Dayuan's troops were weak: truly, with no more than three thousand Han soldiers armed with strong crossbows to shoot them, they could capture and destroy all of Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: Former envoys to Dayuan, such as Yao Dinghan, assured the Emperor that the troops of Dayuan were weak. They claimed that no more than three thousand Han soldiers armed with powerful crossbows would be needed to conquer and destroy the whole of Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 天子已嘗使浞野侯攻樓蘭，以七百騎先至，虜其王，以定漢等言為然，而欲侯寵姬李氏，拜李廣利為貳師將軍，發屬國六千騎，及郡國惡少年數萬人，以往伐宛。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven had previously sent the Marquis of Zhuoye to attack Loulan, who arrived first with seven hundred cavalry and captured their king. He therefore considered Dinghan's words plausible, and also wishing to ennoble Li, the clan of his favorite consort, he appointed Li Guangli as the General of Ershi.
