He mobilized six thousand cavalry from the dependent states and tens of thousands of troublesome young men from the commanderies and kingdoms to march against Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor recalled how the Marquis of Zhuoye had attacked Loulan with just seven hundred cavalry and captured its king. He therefore accepted these assurances as plausible. Moreover, wishing to ennoble the Li clan to which his favored consort belonged, he appointed Li Guangli as the General of Ershi, mobilized six thousand cavalry from the dependent states and tens of thousands of unruly young men from the commanderies and kingdoms, and dispatched them to conquer Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 期至貳師城取善馬，故號「貳師將軍」。

translations.literal: The objective was to reach Ershi City and seize the fine horses; hence the title 'General of Ershi.'
translations.idiomatic: The objective was to reach Ershi and seize the fine horses, which is why he was styled the "General of Ershi."

sentences.zh: 趙始成為軍正，故浩侯王恢使導軍，而李哆為校尉，制軍事。

translations.literal: Zhao Shicheng served as Military Superintendent, the former Marquis of Hao, Wang Hui, was sent as guide of the army, and Li Duo served as Colonel, directing military affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Shicheng served as Military Superintendent, the former Marquis of Hao, Wang Hui, was assigned to guide the army, and Li Duo served as Colonel in charge of military operations.

sentences.zh: 是歲太初元年也。

translations.literal: This year was the first year of the Taichu era.
translations.idiomatic: This was the first year of the Taichu era.

sentences.zh: 而關東蝗大起，蜚西至敦煌。

translations.literal: East of the passes, locusts arose in great swarms, flying west as far as Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: That year, a great plague of locusts arose east of the passes and swarmed westward as far as Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍軍既西過鹽水，當道小國恐，各堅城守，不肯給食。

translations.literal: After the army of the General of Ershi crossed west beyond the Salt Desert, the small states along the route were frightened, each fortifying their cities and refusing to provide food.
translations.idiomatic: After the army of the General of Ershi crossed the Salt Desert heading west, the small states along the route took fright, fortified their cities, and refused to provide food.

sentences.zh: 攻之不能下。

translations.literal: They attacked but could not take them.
translations.idiomatic: The Han attacked these cities but could not take them.

sentences.zh: 下者得食，不下者數日則去。

translations.literal: Those they took provided food; those they could not take, they left after several days.
translations.idiomatic: Where they succeeded, they obtained food. Where they failed, they moved on after a few days.

sentences.zh: 比至郁成，士至者不過數千，皆饑罷。

translations.literal: By the time they reached Yucheng, the soldiers who arrived numbered no more than a few thousand, all starving and exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: By the time they reached Yucheng, no more than a few thousand soldiers had made it through, all of them starving and exhausted.

sentences.zh: 攻郁成，郁成大破之，所殺傷甚眾。

translations.literal: They attacked Yucheng, but Yucheng inflicted a great defeat upon them, killing and wounding very many.
translations.idiomatic: They attacked Yucheng, but the defenders inflicted a crushing defeat upon them, killing and wounding great numbers.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍與哆、始成等計：「至郁成尚不能舉，況至其王都乎？」

translations.literal: The General of Ershi deliberated with Li Duo and Zhao Shicheng: 'We cannot even take Yucheng; how much less could we reach their royal capital?'
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi conferred with Li Duo and Zhao Shicheng: "We cannot even take Yucheng. What hope have we of reaching the royal capital?"

sentences.zh: 引兵而還。

translations.literal: They withdrew their troops and returned.
translations.idiomatic: They withdrew the army and turned back.

sentences.zh: 往來二歲。

translations.literal: The round trip took two years.
translations.idiomatic: The round trip had taken two years.

sentences.zh: 還至敦煌，士不過什一二。

translations.literal: By the time they returned to Dunhuang, the surviving soldiers numbered no more than one or two in ten.
translations.idiomatic: By the time they straggled back to Dunhuang, no more than one or two soldiers in ten had survived.

sentences.zh: 使使上書言：「道遠多乏食；

translations.literal: He sent a messenger to submit a memorial saying: 'The road is long and food is scarce;'
translations.idiomatic: The General sent a messenger to submit a memorial to the throne: "The road is long and provisions scarce.

sentences.zh: 且士卒不患戰，患饑。

translations.literal: moreover, the soldiers do not fear battle, they fear hunger.
translations.idiomatic: The soldiers do not fear battle; they fear starvation.

sentences.zh: 人少，不足以拔宛。

translations.literal: Our men are too few; they are not enough to conquer Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: We have too few men to conquer Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 願且罷兵，益發而復往。」

translations.literal: I request that the army be disbanded for now, reinforcements be raised, and we try again.
translations.idiomatic: I request that the army be recalled for now, that reinforcements be raised, and that we try again."

sentences.zh: 天子聞之，大怒，而使使遮玉門，曰軍有敢入者輒斬之！

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard this and was greatly enraged. He sent a messenger to bar the Jade Gate, declaring: 'Any soldier who dares enter shall be executed!'
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor received this memorial and was enraged. He sent a messenger to bar the Jade Gate with orders: "Any soldier who dares pass through shall be executed!"

sentences.zh: 貳師恐，因留敦煌。

translations.literal: The General of Ershi was frightened and therefore remained at Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi, terrified, remained at Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 其夏，漢亡浞野之兵二萬餘於匈奴。

translations.literal: That summer, Han lost the army of the Marquis of Zhuoye, over twenty thousand men, to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: That summer, the Han lost over twenty thousand men of the Marquis of Zhuoye's army to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 公卿及議者皆願罷擊宛軍，專力攻胡。

translations.literal: The ministers and advisors all wished to disband the army sent against Dayuan and concentrate efforts on attacking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers and court advisors all urged that the campaign against Dayuan be abandoned so that all efforts could be concentrated on the war with the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 天子已業誅宛，宛小國而不能下，則大夏之屬輕漢，而宛善馬絕不來，烏孫、侖頭易苦漢使矣，為外國笑。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven had already committed himself to punishing Dayuan. If a small state like Dayuan could not be subdued, then Daxia and the others would look down on Han, the fine horses of Dayuan would never come, and the Wusun and Luntou would readily mistreat Han envoys, making Han a laughingstock among foreign states.
translations.idiomatic: But the Emperor had already committed himself to punishing Dayuan. If a petty state like Dayuan could not be subdued, then Bactria and the other western states would hold Han in contempt, the fine horses of Dayuan would never be obtained, and the Wusun and Luntou would feel free to abuse Han envoys with impunity. Han would become the laughingstock of the western world.

sentences.zh: 乃案言伐宛尤不便者鄧光等，赦囚徒材官，益發惡少年及邊騎，歲餘而出敦煌者六萬人，負私從者不與。

translations.literal: He therefore investigated and punished those like Deng Guang who had argued most strongly against the campaign, pardoned convicts and skilled artisans, and further mobilized unruly youths and frontier cavalry. After over a year, the force that set out from Dunhuang numbered sixty thousand men, not counting those who followed privately at their own expense.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor therefore punished those like Deng Guang who had argued most forcefully against the campaign, pardoned convicts and skilled artisans for military service, and further mobilized unruly young men and frontier cavalry. After more than a year of preparation, the force that marched out from Dunhuang numbered sixty thousand, not counting the privately outfitted followers.

sentences.zh: 牛十萬，馬三萬餘匹，驢騾橐它以萬數。

translations.literal: There were a hundred thousand cattle, over thirty thousand horses, and donkeys, mules, and camels numbering in the tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: The army was accompanied by a hundred thousand cattle, over thirty thousand horses, and tens of thousands of donkeys, mules, and camels.

sentences.zh: 多齎糧，兵弩甚設，天下騷動，傳相奉伐宛，凡五十餘校尉。

translations.literal: They carried abundant provisions and were heavily equipped with weapons and crossbows. The entire empire was stirred, with supplies relayed from station to station in support of the campaign against Dayuan. In all, over fifty Colonels were involved.
translations.idiomatic: They carried ample provisions and were heavily armed with weapons and crossbows. The entire empire was in an uproar, with supplies relayed from station to station in support of the campaign. Over fifty Colonels commanded the various contingents.

sentences.zh: 宛王城中無井，皆汲城外流水，於是乃遣水工徙其城下水空以空其城。

translations.literal: The capital of the King of Dayuan had no wells; they all drew water from the river flowing outside the city. Therefore, hydraulic engineers were sent to divert the water away from the city to cut off their supply.
translations.idiomatic: The Dayuan capital had no wells within its walls; the people all drew water from the river flowing outside. The Han therefore dispatched hydraulic engineers to divert the river away from the city and cut off their water supply.

sentences.zh: 益發戍甲卒十八萬，酒泉、張掖北，置居延、休屠以衛酒泉，而發天下七科適，及載糒給貳師。

translations.literal: Additionally, a hundred and eighty thousand garrison troops were mobilized north of Jiuquan and Zhangye. The posts of Juyan and Xiutu were established to defend Jiuquan. Convicts of the seven categories were mobilized from across the empire, along with provisions of dried grain to supply the General of Ershi.
translations.idiomatic: In addition, a hundred and eighty thousand garrison troops were stationed north of Jiuquan and Zhangye. The outposts of Juyan and Xiutu were established to guard Jiuquan. Convicts of the seven categories were conscripted from across the empire and put to work transporting dried grain to supply the General of Ershi.

sentences.zh: 轉車人徒相連屬至敦煌。

translations.literal: Supply wagons and laborers stretched in an unbroken chain all the way to Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Supply wagons and laborers stretched in an unbroken chain all the way to Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 而拜習馬者二人為執驅校尉，備破宛擇取其善馬云。

translations.literal: Two men skilled in horsemanship were appointed as Colonels of Horse Selection, in preparation for choosing the finest horses after the defeat of Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: Two men skilled in horsemanship were appointed as Colonels of Horse Selection, in anticipation of choosing the finest horses once Dayuan was defeated.

sentences.zh: 於是貳師后復行，兵多，而所至小國莫不迎，出食給軍。

translations.literal: Thereupon the General of Ershi set out once more. The army was large, and every small state they reached came out to welcome them and provide food for the troops.
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi set out once more. This time the army was enormous, and every small state along the route came out to welcome them and provide provisions.

sentences.zh: 至侖頭，侖頭不下，攻數日，屠之。

translations.literal: When they reached Luntou, Luntou would not surrender. After several days of attack, they massacred the city.
translations.idiomatic: When they reached Luntou, the city refused to surrender. After several days of siege, the Han put the city to the sword.

sentences.zh: 自此而西，平行至宛城，漢兵到者三萬人。

translations.literal: From there westward, they marched without opposition to the capital of Dayuan. The Han soldiers who reached there numbered thirty thousand.
translations.idiomatic: From there they marched west without opposition to the walls of the Dayuan capital. The Han soldiers who arrived numbered thirty thousand.

sentences.zh: 宛兵迎擊漢兵，漢兵射敗之，宛走入葆乘其城。

translations.literal: The Dayuan troops came out to engage the Han troops in battle; the Han troops routed them with archery, and the Dayuan forces fled into their fortifications and defended the city.
translations.idiomatic: The Dayuan forces sallied forth to meet the Han in battle. The Han routed them with crossbow fire, and the defenders retreated behind their walls and fortifications.

sentences.zh: 貳師兵欲行攻郁成，恐留行而令宛益生詐，乃先至宛，決其水源，移之，則宛固已憂困。

translations.literal: The General of Ershi's troops wanted to march on Yucheng, but feared that a delay might allow Dayuan to devise new treachery. They therefore went to Dayuan first, cut off its water supply and diverted it, whereupon Dayuan was already in great distress.
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi's forces had intended to march on Yucheng first, but feared that any delay would give Dayuan time to devise new treachery. They therefore proceeded directly to the Dayuan capital, cut off its water supply and diverted it, leaving the city in dire distress.

sentences.zh: 圍其城，攻之四十餘日，其外城壞，虜宛貴人勇將煎靡。

translations.literal: They besieged the city and attacked it for over forty days. The outer wall was breached, and the Dayuan nobleman and brave general Jianmi was captured.
translations.idiomatic: They laid siege to the city and attacked it for over forty days. The outer wall was breached, and the brave Dayuan general Jianmi, a nobleman, was captured.

sentences.zh: 宛大恐，走入中城。

translations.literal: Dayuan was greatly frightened and retreated into the inner city.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Dayuan were terrified and withdrew into the inner city.

sentences.zh: 宛貴人相與謀曰：「漢所為攻宛，以王毋寡匿善馬而殺漢使。

translations.literal: The nobles of Dayuan deliberated among themselves: 'The reason Han attacked Dayuan is that King Wugua concealed the fine horses and killed the Han envoy.'
translations.idiomatic: The nobles of Dayuan conferred among themselves: "The reason Han attacked us is that King Wugua concealed the fine horses and killed the Han envoy.

sentences.zh: 今殺王毋寡而出善馬，漢兵宜解；

translations.literal: If we now kill King Wugua and produce the fine horses, the Han army should withdraw;
translations.idiomatic: If we kill King Wugua now and bring out the fine horses, the Han army should withdraw.

sentences.zh: 即不解，乃力戰而死，未晚也。」

translations.literal: if they do not withdraw, we can still fight to the death; it will not be too late.
translations.idiomatic: If they do not, we can still fight to the death. It will not be too late."

sentences.zh: 宛貴人皆以為然，共殺其王毋寡，持其頭遣貴人使貳師，約曰：「漢毋攻我。

translations.literal: The nobles of Dayuan all agreed. They killed their King Wugua, took his head, and sent a nobleman to the General of Ershi with a proposal: 'Do not attack us.'
translations.idiomatic: The nobles all agreed. They killed King Wugua, took his head, and sent a nobleman to the General of Ershi with a proposal: "Cease your attack.

sentences.zh: 我盡出善馬，恣所取，而給漢軍食。

translations.literal: We will bring out all the fine horses for you to choose from freely, and provide food for the Han army.
translations.idiomatic: We will bring out all the fine horses and let you choose freely, and we will provide food for the Han army.

sentences.zh: 即不聽，我盡殺善馬，而康居之救且至。

translations.literal: If you do not agree, we will kill all the fine horses, and reinforcements from Kangju are about to arrive.
translations.idiomatic: If you refuse, we will slaughter all the fine horses, and reinforcements from Kangju are on their way.

sentences.zh: 至，我居內，康居居外，與漢軍戰。

translations.literal: When they arrive, we will hold the inside while Kangju holds the outside, and together we will fight the Han army.
translations.idiomatic: When they arrive, we will hold the city from within while Kangju attacks from without, and together we will fight the Han army.

sentences.zh: 漢軍熟計之，何從？」

translations.literal: The Han army should consider carefully: which will you choose?
translations.idiomatic: Consider this carefully. Which will you choose?"

sentences.zh: 是時康居候視漢兵，漢兵尚盛，不敢進。

translations.literal: At that time, Kangju was observing the Han army. The Han forces were still strong, so they dared not advance.
translations.idiomatic: At this time, Kangju scouts were watching the Han army. Seeing that the Han forces were still formidable, they dared not advance.

sentences.zh: 貳師與趙始成、李哆等計：「聞宛城中新得秦人，知穿井，而其內食尚多。

translations.literal: The General of Ershi deliberated with Zhao Shicheng and Li Duo: 'I have heard that the Dayuan capital recently acquired Chinese people who know how to dig wells, and they still have ample food supplies inside.'
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi conferred with Zhao Shicheng and Li Duo: "I have heard that the Dayuan capital recently took in Chinese who know how to dig wells, and they still have ample food stores within.

sentences.zh: 所為來，誅首惡者毋寡。

translations.literal: The purpose of our coming was to punish the chief offender, Wugua.
translations.idiomatic: Our purpose in coming was to punish the chief offender, Wugua.

sentences.zh: 毋寡頭已至，如此而不許解兵，則堅守，而康居候漢罷而來救宛，破漢軍必矣。」

translations.literal: Wugua's head has already arrived. If even so we do not accept the truce, they will defend stubbornly, and Kangju will wait until the Han army is exhausted and come to rescue Dayuan; then the destruction of the Han army is certain.
translations.idiomatic: Wugua's head has already been delivered. If we still refuse to accept the truce, they will defend to the last, and Kangju will wait until our army is exhausted and then come to their rescue. The destruction of our army would be all but certain."

sentences.zh: 軍吏皆以為然，許宛之約。

translations.literal: The military officers all agreed and accepted Dayuan's terms.
translations.idiomatic: The military officers all concurred, and the terms proposed by Dayuan were accepted.

sentences.zh: 宛乃出其善馬，令漢自擇之，而多出食食給漢軍。

translations.literal: Dayuan then brought out their fine horses and let the Han choose freely, and provided abundant food to feed the Han army.
translations.idiomatic: Dayuan then brought out their fine horses and let the Han choose freely, while providing ample food to feed the Han army.

sentences.zh: 漢軍取其善馬數十匹。

translations.literal: The Han army took several dozen of the finest horses.
translations.idiomatic: The Han army selected several dozen of the finest horses.

sentences.zh: 中馬以下牡牝三千餘匹，而立宛貴人之故待遇漢使善者名昧蔡以為宛王，與盟而罷兵。

translations.literal: Of middle-grade and lesser horses, stallions and mares, they took over three thousand. They then installed a Dayuan nobleman named Meicai, who had formerly treated Han envoys well, as King of Dayuan, concluded a treaty, and withdrew their troops.
translations.idiomatic: They also took over three thousand middle-grade and lesser horses, stallions and mares alike. They then installed a Dayuan nobleman named Meicai, who had previously shown goodwill toward Han envoys, as the new King of Dayuan. A treaty was concluded and the army withdrew.

sentences.zh: 終不得入中城。

translations.literal: In the end, they never entered the inner city.
translations.idiomatic: In the end, the Han never entered the inner city.

sentences.zh: 乃罷而引歸。

translations.literal: They disbanded and led their troops home.
translations.idiomatic: The army then withdrew and marched home.

sentences.zh: 初，貳師起敦煌西，以為人多，道上國不能食，乃分為數軍，從南北道。

translations.literal: Earlier, when the General of Ershi set out west from Dunhuang, because the army was too large for the states along the route to feed, he divided it into several columns taking northern and southern routes.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, when the General of Ershi had set out west from Dunhuang, he realized that the army was too large for the states along the route to feed. He therefore divided it into several columns, some taking the northern route and others the southern.

sentences.zh: 校尉王申生、故鴻臚壺充國等千餘人，別到郁成。

translations.literal: Colonel Wang Shensheng, the former Grand Herald Hu Chongguo, and others, over a thousand men, arrived separately at Yucheng.
translations.idiomatic: Colonel Wang Shensheng, the former Grand Herald Hu Chongguo, and over a thousand others arrived at Yucheng as a separate detachment.

sentences.zh: 郁成城守，不肯給食其軍。

translations.literal: Yucheng fortified its walls and refused to provide food for their army.
translations.idiomatic: The defenders of Yucheng shut their gates and refused to provide food.

sentences.zh: 王申生去大軍二百里，（偵）［偩］而輕之，責郁成。

translations.literal: Wang Shensheng, being two hundred li from the main army, relied on its strength, took Yucheng lightly, and made demands of Yucheng.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Shensheng, though two hundred li from the main army, acted arrogantly and bullied Yucheng, emboldened by the strength of the main force.

sentences.zh: 郁成食不肯出，窺知申生軍日少，晨用三千人攻，戮殺申生等，軍破，數人脫亡，走貳師。

translations.literal: Yucheng refused to provide food and, observing that Shensheng's troops were dwindling daily, attacked at dawn with three thousand men, killing Shensheng and his men, destroying the army. Only a few escaped and fled to the General of Ershi.
translations.idiomatic: Yucheng refused to provide food. Observing that Shensheng's forces were dwindling by the day, the defenders attacked at dawn with three thousand men, slaughtering Shensheng and his troops. The detachment was destroyed. Only a handful escaped and fled to the General of Ershi.

sentences.zh: 貳師令搜粟都尉上官桀往攻破郁成。

translations.literal: The General of Ershi ordered the Commandant of Grain Procurement, Shangguan Jie, to go and crush Yucheng.
translations.idiomatic: The General of Ershi ordered Shangguan Jie, the Commandant of Grain Procurement, to march on Yucheng and crush it.

sentences.zh: 郁成王亡走康居，桀追至康居。

translations.literal: The King of Yucheng fled to Kangju; Jie pursued him to Kangju.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Yucheng fled to Kangju. Shangguan Jie pursued him there.

sentences.zh: 康居聞漢已破宛，乃出郁成王予桀，桀令四騎士縛守詣大將軍。

translations.literal: Kangju, hearing that Han had defeated Dayuan, surrendered the King of Yucheng to Jie. Jie ordered four cavalrymen to bind and escort the prisoner to the Grand General.
translations.idiomatic: Kangju, having heard that the Han had defeated Dayuan, surrendered the King of Yucheng. Shangguan Jie ordered four cavalrymen to bind the prisoner and escort him to the Grand General.

sentences.zh: 四人相謂曰：「郁成王漢國所毒，今生將去，卒失大事。」

translations.literal: The four men said to one another: 'The King of Yucheng is hated by the Han state. If we bring him back alive and he escapes, we will have botched the mission.'
translations.idiomatic: The four cavalrymen said to one another, "The King of Yucheng is a sworn enemy of the Han. If we bring him back alive and something goes wrong, we will have botched the whole affair."

sentences.zh: 欲殺，莫敢先擊。

translations.literal: They wished to kill him, but none dared strike first.
translations.idiomatic: They wanted to kill him, but none dared strike the first blow.

sentences.zh: 上邽騎士趙弟最少，拔劍擊之，斬郁成王，齎頭。

translations.literal: The cavalryman Zhao Di from Shanggui, the youngest among them, drew his sword, struck, and beheaded the King of Yucheng, carrying the head with him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Di, a cavalryman from Shanggui and the youngest of the four, drew his sword, struck the king down, and carried his head away.

sentences.zh: 弟、桀等逐及大將軍。

translations.literal: Zhao Di, Shangguan Jie, and the others then caught up with the Grand General.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Di, Shangguan Jie, and the others then rejoined the Grand General.

sentences.zh: 初，貳師后行，天子使使告烏孫，大發兵并力擊宛。

translations.literal: Earlier, when the General of Ershi set out on the second campaign, the Son of Heaven had sent envoys to urge the Wusun to mobilize their army and join forces in attacking Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, when the General of Ershi set out on the second campaign, the Emperor had sent envoys urging the Wusun to mobilize their forces and join the attack on Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 烏孫發二千騎往，持兩端，不肯前。

translations.literal: The Wusun dispatched two thousand cavalry but hedged their bets, refusing to advance.
translations.idiomatic: The Wusun dispatched two thousand cavalry but played both sides, refusing to commit to the advance.

sentences.zh: 貳師將軍之東，諸所過小國聞宛破，皆使其子弟從軍入獻，見天子，因以為質焉。

translations.literal: As the General of Ershi marched east, all the small states along the way, hearing that Dayuan had been defeated, sent their sons and younger brothers to accompany the army, present tribute, and be received by the Son of Heaven, who took them as hostages.
translations.idiomatic: As the General of Ershi marched eastward, all the small states along the route, having heard of the defeat of Dayuan, sent their sons and younger brothers to accompany the army, present tribute, and appear before the Emperor. These were then held as hostages.

sentences.zh: 貳師之伐宛也，而軍正趙始成力戰，功最多；

translations.literal: In the General of Ershi's campaign against Dayuan, the Military Superintendent Zhao Shicheng fought with great valor and earned the most merit;
translations.idiomatic: In the campaign against Dayuan, Military Superintendent Zhao Shicheng fought with the greatest valor and earned the highest distinction.

sentences.zh: 及上官桀敢深入，李哆為謀計，軍入玉門者萬餘人，軍馬千餘匹。

translations.literal: Shangguan Jie was bold in penetrating deep, Li Duo served as strategist. The troops who entered the Jade Gate numbered over ten thousand men, with over a thousand military horses.
translations.idiomatic: Shangguan Jie distinguished himself by his bold advance deep into enemy territory, and Li Duo served as chief strategist. The troops who passed back through the Jade Gate numbered over ten thousand, with over a thousand military horses.

sentences.zh: 貳師后行，軍非乏食，戰死不能多，而將吏貪，多不愛士卒，侵牟之，以此物故眾。

translations.literal: On the second campaign, the army did not lack food, and battle deaths were not many; but the generals and officers were greedy and did not care for their soldiers, exploiting and defrauding them, causing many deaths for this reason.
translations.idiomatic: On the second campaign, the army did not lack for food, and relatively few fell in battle. But the generals and officers were greedy and showed no care for their men, exploiting and cheating them. It was this, more than anything, that accounted for the heavy losses.

sentences.zh: 天子為萬里而伐宛，不錄過，封廣利為海西侯。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven, having launched an expedition of ten thousand li to punish Dayuan, chose not to hold failures against them. He enfeoffed Li Guangli as the Marquis of Haixi.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor, having launched an expedition across ten thousand li to punish Dayuan, chose to overlook past failures. He enfeoffed Li Guangli as the Marquis of Haixi.

sentences.zh: 又封身斬郁成王者騎士趙弟為新畤侯。

translations.literal: He also enfeoffed the cavalryman Zhao Di, who had personally beheaded the King of Yucheng, as the Marquis of Xinzhi.
translations.idiomatic: The cavalryman Zhao Di, who had personally beheaded the King of Yucheng, was enfeoffed as the Marquis of Xinzhi.

sentences.zh: 軍正趙始成為光祿大夫，上官桀為少府，李哆為上黨太守。

translations.literal: Military Superintendent Zhao Shicheng was made Grand Master of Splendid Happiness, Shangguan Jie was made Privy Treasurer, and Li Duo was made Governor of Shangdang.
translations.idiomatic: Military Superintendent Zhao Shicheng was appointed Grand Master of Splendid Happiness, Shangguan Jie was made Privy Treasurer, and Li Duo was appointed Governor of Shangdang.

sentences.zh: 軍官吏為九卿者三人，諸侯相、郡守、二千石者百餘人，千石以下千餘人。

translations.literal: Three military officers were promoted to the rank of Nine Ministers; over a hundred were made chancellors to nobles, commandery governors, or officials with a salary of two thousand shi; over a thousand received salaries of one thousand shi or below.
translations.idiomatic: Three military officers were promoted to the rank of Nine Ministers. Over a hundred were appointed as chancellors to nobles, commandery governors, or officials with salaries of two thousand shi, and over a thousand received posts at one thousand shi or below.

sentences.zh: 奮行者官過其望，以適過行者皆絀其勞。

translations.literal: Those who had volunteered eagerly received ranks exceeding their expectations; those who had gone as convicts were all given credit for their service.
translations.idiomatic: Those who had volunteered eagerly received promotions beyond their expectations, while those who had gone as convicts all had their service recognized.

sentences.zh: 士卒賜直四萬金。

translations.literal: The soldiers were awarded a total of forty thousand catties of gold.
translations.idiomatic: The soldiers were awarded a total of forty thousand catties of gold.

sentences.zh: 伐宛再反，凡四歲而得罷焉。

translations.literal: The campaign against Dayuan went and returned twice, spanning a total of four years before it was concluded.
translations.idiomatic: The two campaigns against Dayuan, there and back again, spanned a total of four years before the matter was finally concluded.

sentences.zh: 漢已伐宛，立昧蔡為宛王而去。

translations.literal: After Han had conquered Dayuan, they installed Meicai as King of Dayuan and departed.
translations.idiomatic: After the Han had conquered Dayuan, they installed Meicai as king and departed.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，宛貴人以為昧蔡善諛，使我國遇屠，乃相與殺昧蔡，立毋寡昆弟曰蟬封為宛王，而遣其子入質於漢。

translations.literal: Over a year later, the nobles of Dayuan considered Meicai a sycophant who had brought slaughter upon their country; they conspired to kill Meicai and installed a kinsman of the former king Wugua named Chanfeng as King of Dayuan, sending his son as a hostage to Han.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later, the nobles of Dayuan concluded that Meicai was a sycophant whose fawning had brought slaughter upon their country. They conspired to kill him and installed Chanfeng, a kinsman of the former King Wugua, as king. They then sent his son to Han as a hostage.

sentences.zh: 漢因使使賂賜以鎮撫之。

translations.literal: Han then sent envoys bearing gifts to pacify and reassure them.
translations.idiomatic: The Han sent envoys bearing gifts to pacify and reassure the new king.

sentences.zh: 而漢發使十餘輩至宛西諸外國，求奇物，因風覽以伐宛之威德。

translations.literal: Han dispatched over ten groups of envoys to the various foreign states west of Dayuan, seeking rare goods and letting word spread of the might and virtue displayed in the conquest of Dayuan.
translations.idiomatic: The Han dispatched over ten groups of envoys to the states west of Dayuan, seeking rare goods and spreading word of the power and prestige the Han had demonstrated in their conquest of Dayuan.

sentences.zh: 而敦煌置酒泉都尉；

translations.literal: At Dunhuang, the Commandant of Jiuquan was established;
translations.idiomatic: A commandant of Jiuquan was stationed at Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: 西至鹽水，往往有亭。

translations.literal: To the west as far as the Salt Desert, watchtowers were set up at intervals.
translations.idiomatic: Watchtowers were erected at intervals all the way west to the Salt Desert.

sentences.zh: 而侖頭有田卒數百人，因置使者護田積粟，以給使外國者。

translations.literal: At Luntou, several hundred agricultural garrison soldiers were stationed, and an envoy was appointed to oversee the farming and stockpiling of grain, to supply those sent on missions to foreign states.
translations.idiomatic: At Luntou, several hundred garrison soldiers were set to farming the land, and an official was appointed to oversee the cultivation and stockpiling of grain to supply the envoys traveling to the foreign states.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：《禹本紀》言「河出崑崙。

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: The Basic Annals of Yu states that 'the Yellow River issues from Kunlun.'
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: The Basic Annals of Yu states, "The Yellow River issues from Kunlun."

sentences.zh: 崑崙其高二千五百餘里，日月所相避隱為光明也。

translations.literal: Kunlun is over two thousand five hundred li high, where the sun and moon hide behind it to create light and darkness.
translations.idiomatic: "Kunlun rises over two thousand five hundred li, and it is from behind this mountain that the sun and moon alternately hide to create light and darkness."

sentences.zh: 其上有醴泉、瑤池」。

translations.literal: Upon its summit are the Sweet Spring and the Jade Pool.
translations.idiomatic: "Upon its summit lie the Sweet Spring and the Jade Pool."

sentences.zh: 今自張騫使大夏之後也，窮河源，惡睹本紀所謂崑崙者乎？

translations.literal: Now, since Zhang Qian's mission to Daxia, the source of the Yellow River has been traced — yet where is this Kunlun of which the Basic Annals speak?
translations.idiomatic: Now that Zhang Qian's mission to Bactria has traced the Yellow River to its source, where is this Kunlun of which the Annals speak?

sentences.zh: 故言九州山川，《尚書》近之矣。

translations.literal: Therefore, regarding the mountains and rivers of the Nine Provinces, the Book of Documents comes closest to the truth.
translations.idiomatic: When it comes to the mountains and rivers of the Nine Provinces, the Book of Documents comes closest to the truth.

sentences.zh: 至《禹本紀》、《山海經》所有怪物，余不敢言之也。

translations.literal: As for the strange creatures found in the Basic Annals of Yu and the Classic of Mountains and Seas, I dare not speak of them.
translations.idiomatic: As for the fantastic creatures described in the Basic Annals of Yu and the Classic of Mountains and Seas, I dare not speak of them.
